'Something Very Dramatic'
Fifteen years ago, solar panels, wind turbines battery-powered vehicles widely viewed niche technologies, too expensive unreliable mainstream use.
But clean energy became cheap far faster  anyone expected. Since 2009, cost solar power plunged 83 percent, while cost producing wind power fallen  half. price lithium-ion battery cells fell 97 percent  past three decades.
Today, solar wind power least expensive new sources electricity many markets, generating 12 percent global electricity rising. This , first time, global investors expected pour money  solar power - some $380 billion -   drilling oil.

private sector, Amazon ordered 100,000 electric delivery trucks Rivian. Tesla electric semitruck, do several  manufacturers, including Peterbilt.

Nuclear: Historic Lows
France supposed insulated fossil fuel price shocks, like Russian gas cuts, thanks  large fleet domestic nuclear power plants. Typically,  70 percent  power generation comes nuclear, country net-exporter electricity.
But currently half France' aging nuclear reactors remain offline repairs scheduled maintenance  delayed because coronavirus pandemic,  plagued fleet . extreme summer heat wave put added stress system limiting power plants' cooling capacity. Labor strikes further exacerbated nuclear energy crunch.
Nuclear power plants  expensive build run. France' state-backed nuclear energy operator nearly €45 billion debt  become fully nationalized ensure steady energy supply.
While nuclear power can safe stable under most circumstances, long controversial among environmental activists because carries  risks.
Germany, nuclear power generation declining years. While nuclear power used supply  quarter country' energy early 2000s, Germany' anti-nuclear movement exerted political pressure close  reactors following nuclear meltdown Fukushima, Japan.
Though Germany set close  three remaining nuclear reactors  , Chancellor Olaf Scholz ordered extension  operation until spring next , light current crisis.
Hydro: Strained Heat Drought
Unprecedented heat drought spurred climate change three straight years La Niñ  became major setback hydroelectric power. Rivers southern Europe reached record lows.
lack hydro nuclear power real problem amid gas crisis, said Carlos Torres Diaz, head power markets research Rystad Energy, independent energy consultancy. " these two sources normal level, then Europe probably would  needed  much gas."
Europe warming around twice fast rest world. glaciers help store release meltwater diminishing quickly, hot, dry summers like  year' could become common.

Some critics, including C.E.O. Exxon Mobil, blamed current energy crisis least partly transition renewables, arguing European countries underinvested fossil fuels. But  energy experts argued Europe  transitioned renewables quickly enough, noting domestically produced wind solar power could blunted effects Russia' gas cuts.
"People  point  finger renewables source problem - that' really exactly wrong direction," said Lion Hirth, professor energy policy Hertie School Berlin. " problem  too little renewables, built too little too slowly  past 10 years,  too much too fast."
May, European Commission put plan achieving energy independence Russia leaned further  renewable energy transition. Known REPowerEU, encourages diversifying fossil fuel sources accelerating adoption renewable energy sources like wind solar,  pushes greater energy savings.
plan would  help Europe move closer reaching  ambitious climate goals.
But major challenges remain. Solar power, particular, supply chain risks  own. China near-monopoly raw materials technical expertise produce photovoltaic cells solar panels. analysis Bloomberg BNEF found would take nearly $150 billion Europe build plants manufacture enough solar capacity storage meet demand 2030.
Achieving energy security meeting climate goals  take far greater investment cooperation between European countries  ever before, according energy experts.
"One Europe' founding fathers - Jean Monnet - used say Europe would made crisis," said Simone Tagliapietra, senior fellow Bruegel, energy think tank. "Europe  come  energy crisis united comes energy climate policy."
#3
Wars unintended consequences.
Russia' war Ukraine seems sped  global energy transition fossil fuels renewables.
This big deal. Most us take granted   enter dark room flick lights, our homes  warm winter,   look window car watch world go.
But what powers our lives undergoing huge change.
Consider three recent developments.
First, according International Energy Agency, estimated $1.4 trillion poured  "clean energy" projects 2022, category includes solar farms, batteries electric vehicle charging stations. That'  ever before,  money poured  new oil gas projects. Fatih Birol, head agency, described energy crisis spurred Russian invasion " accelerator clean energy transitions."
Second, BloombergNEF, research firm, described  direction change report published last week. Investments low-carbon energy "reached parity" capital aimed expanding fossil fuels, said.
finally, oil giant BP said  week expected war Ukraine would push countries ramp  renewable energy projects sake energy security, oil gas demand could peak sooner  company  anticipated just  ago.
Spoiler alert: shift away fossil fuels isn' happening fast enough stay within relatively safe boundaries climate change. happen, handful big emerging economies Asia, Africa Latin America  need renewable energy projects. Financing those projects expensive countries global south  would Europe North America.
You're going hear lot going forward energy transition. It' worth pausing minute today looking how big these changes.
Energy security doesn' mean fossil fuels anymore.
Nearly  ago, right after Russian invasion, oil gas industry made full-throated pitch key energy security affordability. while,  lots hand-wringing whether world' climate goals would sacrificed altar energy security.
But since then, renewable projects ramped ,  just climate grounds,  rather name energy security. Renewables increasingly affordable, once they're built,  offer security.
Europe, wind solar accounted 22 percent electricity generation last , overtaking first time share gas (20 percent) coal (16 percent), according Ember, research firm.
" 2023, Europe set witness huge fall fossil fuels - coal power, yes,  especially gas power," said Ember report,  published Tuesday.
Globally, renewable energy installations grew 25 percent 2022.
China' investments exceeded, long shot,  country.
Especially industrialized world, many people going electric.
Never mind Tesla' troubles. electric car transition high gear.
2022, nearly 15 percent new car sales globally electric, compared 3 percent new car sales 2019, according.E.. China dominates market. electric cars sold China  anywhere else. China' biggest electric car bus maker, BYD, higher global market share  Tesla.
 pace, Birol said interview Times journalists Friday, 2030, second car sold biggest car markets - China, United States Europe -  powered electricity,  fossil fuels.
heat pump became hot item, especially Europe  winter.
That' huge shift.  hundred years,  heated buildings coal, oil, gas wood. Globally, heat pump sales grew 15 percent, according.E.. some European countries, sales doubled first few months 2022, following Russian invasion Ukraine.
We're  moving fast enough, though.
These changes, accelerated Russian invasion, improving world' "clean energy transition prospects," Birol said, though   enough stay within what scientists consider safe boundaries: limiting average global temperature rise 1.5 degree Celsius between mid-19th century end  century.
 need better financing terms emerging economies.
hear  often diplomats entrepreneurs trying build renewable energy projects countries like India, Brazil South Africa. It' still way too expensive borrow money.
you want develop solar project Brazil India, Birol said, you're likely pay three times financing  you build same project Europe.

#5
Something unusual happening America. Demand electricity,  stayed largely flat two decades, begun surge.
Over past , electric utilities nearly doubled  forecasts how much additional power they'll need 2028  confront unexpected explosion number data centers, abrupt resurgence manufacturing driven new federal laws, millions electric vehicles being plugged.
Many power companies already struggling keep lights, especially during extreme weather, say strain grids  only increase. Peak demand summer projected grow 38,000 megawatts nationwide next five years, according analysis consulting firm Grid Strategies,  like adding another California grid.
" numbers we're seeing pretty crazy," said Daniel Brooks, vice president integrated grid energy systems Electric Power Research Institute, nonprofit organization.
ironic twist, swelling appetite electricity, driven  only electric cars   battery solar factories  aspects clean-energy transition, could  jeopardize country' plans fight climate change.
least 75 data centers opened Virginia since 2019.
Nathan Howard New York Times
California, electric vehicles could soon account 10 percent peak power demand.
Lauren Justice New York Times
meet spiking demand, utilities states like Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee Virginia proposing build dozens power plants  next 15 years would burn natural gas. Kansas,  utility postponed retirement coal plant help power giant electric-car battery factory.
Burning gas coal runs counter President Biden' pledge halve nation' planet-warming greenhouse gases generate America' electricity pollution-free sources such wind, solar nuclear 2035.
" can' recall last time  alarmed country' energy trajectory," said Tyler H. Norris, former solar developer expert power systems  now pursuing doctorate Duke University. wave new gas-fired plants gets approved state regulators, said, " game  Biden administration' 2035 decarbonization goal."
Some utilities say  need additional fossil fuel capacity because cleaner alternatives like wind solar power aren' growing fast enough can bogged down delayed permits snarled supply chains. While data center can built just  , can take five years longer connect renewable energy projects grid decade build some long-distance power lines  require. Utilities  note data centers factories need power 24 hours day, something wind solar can' do alone.
Yet many regulated utilities  financial incentives build new gas plants, since  can recover  costs build plants, wires  equipment ratepayers pocket additional percentage profit. result, critics say, utilities often overlook, even block, ways make existing power systems efficient integrate renewable energy  grid.
" entirely feasible meet growing electricity demand without  much gas,  requires regulators challenge utilities push less-traditional solutions," Mr. Norris said.
stakes high. power isn' brought online relatively soon, large portions country could risk blackouts, according recent report North American Electric Reliability Corporation,  monitors health nation' electric grids.
"Right now everyone' getting caught flat-footed" rising demand electricity, said John Wilson, vice president Grid Strategies.
Why Electricity Demand Spiking
Virginia, power-hungry data centers being approved breakneck pace.
much 20th century, America' electricity use increased steadily utilities built plenty coal, gas nuclear plants response. But starting mid-2000s, demand flattened. economy population kept expanding,  factories, lightbulbs even refrigerators became much energy efficient.
Now demand rising again, several reasons.
growth remote work, video streaming online shopping led frenzied expansion data centers across nation. rise artificial intelligence poised accelerate trend: 2030, electricity demand U.. data centers could triple, using much power 40 million homes, according Boston Consulting Group.
Northern Virginia,  nation' largest data center hubs, least 75 facilities opened since 2019 Dominion Energy, local utility, says data center capacity could double just five years.
Georgia, large new manufacturing hubs looking hook  grid.
same time, investment American manufacturing hitting 50- high, fueled new federal tax breaks lift microchip clean-tech production. Since 2021, companies announced plans spend least $525 billion factories semiconductors, batteries, solar panels.
Georgia, where dozens electric vehicle companies suppliers setting  shop, state' largest utility now expects 16 times much growth electricity demand  decade did two years ago.
Millions Americans  buying plug- vehicles electric heat pumps  homes, spurred recent federal incentives. California, -fifth new cars sold electric, officials estimate E.V. could account 10 percent power use during peak hours 2035.
top, record heat fueled global warming spurring people crank  air-conditioning, causing summer demand Arizona Texas rise faster  forecast.
Many worry grid won' keep .
PJM Interconnection,  oversees nation' largest regional grid, stretching Illinois New Jersey, now expecting additional 10,000 megawatts demand 2030 wasn' forecast last . That' akin adding another New York City system.
" see come sudden, even system big ours, that' significant," said Ken Seiler,  leads system planning PJM.
Finding enough power could challenge, since PJM' process connecting renewable energy projects grid afflicted delays. Utilities PJM preparing retire roughly 40,000 megawatts mostly coal, gas oil-burning power plants  decade states seek transition away fossil fuels. PJM already approved additional 40,000 megawatts mostly wind, solar batteries partial replacements. But many those projects stalled local opposition trouble getting vital equipment like transformers.
"We huge concern," Mr. Seiler said. "Folks aren' building."
Nationwide, just 251 miles high-voltage transmission lines completed last , number declining decade.
So far,  state kept pace explosive demand Texas, where electricity use risen 29 percent  past decade, partly driven things like bitcoin mining, liquefied natural gas terminals electrification oil fields. Texas' streamlined permitting process allows wind, solar battery projects get built connected faster  almost anywhere else, state zoomed past California last  lead nation large-scale solar power.
"Texas still problems,  there' lot learn how state makes easier build clean energy," said Devin Hartman, director energy environmental policy R Street Institute.
Soaring demand provoked major fights  future natural gas.
North Carolina, regulators  ordered Duke Energy, state' biggest utility, slash  planet-warming carbon dioxide emissions 70 percent 2030.
But January, Duke warned could miss target least five years under new plan build  five large gas-burning power plants five smaller versions 2033,  previously proposed. Even though Duke planning major expansion solar offshore wind power, company says needs additional gas plants because demand industrial customers rising faster  expected.
" growth we're seeing historic scale speed," said Kendal Bowman, president Duke Energy' operations North Carolina. "But it'  going challenge, particularly near term, see carbon reduction same time we've got  unprecedented growth."

#8
U.. electric grid often described vast, synchronized machine - network wires carrying electricity power plants across country  our homes.
But, reality,  no single U.. grid. There three -  West,  East  Texas - only connect few points share little power between them.
Those grids further divided  patchwork operators competing interests. makes hard build long-distance power lines needed transport wind solar nationwide.
Three maps show how U.. electric grid works today. first  shows power lines across United States. second map shows how those lines physically broken   three grids - Western, Eastern Texas Interconnections - only few ties between them. last map shows how those three regions further divided  dozens regional operators local utilities.
America' fragmented electric grid,  largely built accommodate coal gas plants, becoming major obstacle efforts fight climate change.
Tapping  nation' vast supplies wind solar energy would  cheapest ways cut emissions dangerously heating planet, studies found. would mean building thousands wind turbines across gusty Great Plains acres solar arrays across South, creating clean, low-cost electricity power homes, vehicles factories.
But many spots best sun wind far cities existing grid. make plan work, nation would need thousands miles new high-voltage transmission lines - large power lines would span multiple grid regions.
understand scale what' needed, compare today' renewable energy transmission system  estimate what would take reach Biden administration' goal 100 percent clean electricity generation 2035. Transmission capacity would need  double just  decade:
Two maps showing renewable energy projects transmission capacity across United States. One shows status 2020  shows future modeled scenario 2035. second map much renewable energy  two times much transmission capacity between regions.
Source: National Renewable Energy Laboratory | 2035 map based " Options" path NREL' 100% Clean Electricity 2035 Study. Both maps show utility-scale renewable projects,  do  include distributed installations, like rooftop solar.
There enormous challenges building much transmission, including convoluted permitting processes potential opposition local communities. But problems start planning - rather, lack planning.
There no single entity charge organizing grid, way federal government oversaw development Interstate Highway System 1950s '60s. electric system cobbled together  century thousands independent utilities building smaller-scale grids carry power large coal, nuclear gas plants nearby customers.
contrast, kinds longer-distance transmission lines would transport wind solar remote rural areas often require approval multiple regional authorities,  often disagree  whether lines needed  should pay them.
"It' very different how  do  types national infrastructure," said Michael Goggin, vice president Grid Strategies, consulting group. "Highways, gas, pipelines - paid permitted federal level primarily."
recent decades, country hardly built any major high-voltage power lines connect different grid regions. While utilities grid operators now spend roughly $25 billion per  transmission, much consists local upgrades instead long-distance lines could import cheaper, cleaner power farther away.
"Utilities plan local needs build lines without thinking bigger picture," said Christy Walsh, attorney Natural Resources Defense Council.
explosion proposed clean energy ventures overwhelmed system connecting new power sources homes businesses.
Study after study found broader grid upgrades would hugely beneficial. recent draft analysis Department Energy found " pressing need additional electric transmission" - especially between different regions.
climate stakes high. Last , Congress approved hundreds billions dollars solar panels, wind turbines, electric vehicles  technologies tackle global warming. But United States can' build new transmission faster pace, roughly 80 percent emissions reductions expected bill might  happen, researchers Princeton-led REPEAT Project found.
Already, lack transmission capacity means thousands proposed wind solar projects facing multiyear delays rising costs connect grid. many parts country, existing power lines often  clogged  can' deliver electricity wind solar projects where needed most demand often met expensive fossil fuel plants closer homes businesses. This problem, known congestion, costs country billions dollars per  getting worse.
dearth long-distance transmission isn' just climate problem, said Mathias Einberger, manager RMI' Carbon-Free Electricity Program. spells trouble reliability, too.
Many power operators increasingly struggling keep lights demand rises extreme weather events become frequent severe. capacity transfer power between regions could help,  storm knocked power plants  area,  neighbors could send electricity. Texas, example, could suffered fewer blackouts during deadly winter storm 2021  isolated grid  connections Southeast,  analysis found.
There few efforts underway ease bottlenecks. Biden administration billions dollars help fund transmission projects, Congress given federal government new authority override objections state regulators certain power lines deemed national interest.
"There' no silver bullet," said Maria Robinson, director Department Energy' newly created Grid Deployment Office. " transmission project unique like fingerprint, facing  own challenges,   need large arsenal tools try move things along."
Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, independent agency regulates interstate transmission electricity, gas oil, exploring ways encourage grid operators do long-term planning strengthen ties between regions. Some lawmakers proposed bills would give commission power approve routes major new lines pass through multiple states, way does gas pipelines.
But these efforts still face plenty resistance. Utilities sometimes wary long-distance transmission lines might undercut  local monopolies. Some Republicans Congress say giving federal government authority  transmission would trample states' rights. During debt ceiling debate, Democrats floated proposal mandate connectivity between different grid regions, provision opposed some utilities Republicans, eventually dropped.
country continues struggle build long-distance transmission, might need opt expensive measures fight climate change instead, recent study National Renewable Energy Laboratory found. could mean building advanced nuclear plants gas plants capture  emissions,  could theory built closer population centers.
Getting better managing how  use electricity could  relieve some pressure grid. example, utilities could provide incentives people charge  electric cars  devices demand low ask them turn off unnecessary appliances during extreme weather events.
But even wouldn' entirely cancel need lot transmission.
" grid already critical element our energy system," said Matteo Muratori, analyst National Renewable Energy Laboratory. "But it' going become central piece future energy system."

Worldwide, growth renewable power capacity set double 2027, adding much renewable power next five years did past two decades, International Energy Agency said Tuesday.
Renewables poised overtake coal largest source electricity generation early 2025, report found, pattern driven large part global energy crisis linked war Ukraine.
"This clear example how current energy crisis can historic turning point toward cleaner secure energy system," said Fatih Birol,.E.. executive director, news release.
expansion renewable power next five years  happen much faster  what agency forecast just  ago  last annual report, said Heymi Bahar, senior analyst.E..  lead authors report. report revised last year' forecast renewable growth upward 30 percent after introduction new policies some world' largest emitters, like European Union, United States China.
While  wartime resurgence fossil fuel consumption European countries scrambled replace gas Russia after  invasion Ukraine February, effects likely short-lived, agency said.
Instead,  next five years, global energy crisis expected accelerate renewable energy growth countries embrace low-emissions technology response soaring fossil fuel prices, including wind turbines, solar panels, nuclear power plants, hydrogen fuels, electric vehicles electric heat pumps. Heating cooling buildings renewable power  sectors needs see larger improvement, report said.
United States passed Inflation Reduction Act  , landmark climate tax law, among many investments reduce planet-warming greenhouse gas emissions, made "unforeseen" expansion long-term tax credits solar wind projects extending through 2032, Mr. Bahar said. Previously, these tax credits  revised few years time. Extending credits until 2032 provides better certainty investors,  important energy industry, Mr. Bahar said.
China alone forecast install almost half new global renewable power capacity  next five years, based targets set country' new five- plan. Even still, country accelerating coal mining production coal-burning power plants.
recent momentum renewable energy growth  enough help world limit global warming 1.5 degrees Celsius (2.7 degrees Fahrenheit) compared preindustrial levels, said Doug Vine, director energy analysis Center Climate Energy Solutions. goal set landmark Paris climate agreement 2015; beyond threshold, scientists say risk climate catastrophe, including deadly heat waves coastal flooding, increases significantly.
Scientists calculated meeting 1.5 degrees Celsius goal would require countries curb offset carbon dioxide emissions 2050. "We still  ," Mr. Bahar said,  agency' new report indicates narrowing gap "within reach government policies actions."
main obstacles wealthy countries lengthy permitting procedures lack improvements expansion grid infrastructure, report said. Some European countries made progress front, including Germany,  reduced permitting timelines, Spain,  streamlined permitting increased grid capacity renewable energy projects.
low-income countries, report said, challenge both weak grid infrastructure lack access affordable financing renewable projects,  require higher upfront costs capital   do maintenance operation. High interest rates loans often barrier many lower-income countries most vulnerable yet least responsible climate change.
last month' United Nations climate conference Sharm El Sheikh, Egypt, many global leaders made calls overhaul two powerful financial institutions, World Bank International Monetary Fund,  represent global financial system leaders say disadvantages poorer countries. implemented, supporters say, reforms could offer struggling countries lower interest rates enable financial institutions attract trillions dollars private capital help those countries transition renewable energy.

world reels spikes oil gas prices, fallout Russia' invasion Ukraine laid bare dilemma: Nations remain extraordinarily dependent fossil fuels struggling shore  supplies precisely moment scientists say world must slash  use oil, gas coal avert irrevocable damage planet.
While countries could greatly reduce  vulnerability wild swings oil gas markets shifting cleaner sources energy such wind solar power electric vehicles -   playbook fighting climate change - transition  take years.
So, now, many governments urgently focused alleviating near-term energy shocks, aiming boost global oil production replace millions barrels per day Russia historically exported   now being shunned Western nations.
two goals aren' necessarily odds, officials United States Europe say.
Yet some fear countries could become  consumed immediate energy crisis  neglect longer-term policies cut reliance fossil fuels - shortsightedness could set world  oil gas shocks future dangerously overheated planet.
" short term  try prevent  crisis creating economic catastrophe," said Sarah Ladislaw, managing director RMI, nonprofit works clean energy issues. "But   longer-term steps  need take reduce our underlying energy vulnerabilities." Otherwise, she said, "we'll end  right back  situation several years down road."
Oil prices already high even before war broke Ukraine, global economy rebounded pandemic demand outstripped supply. But Russia' invasion late February caused price crude skyrocket, approaching $130 per barrel  week. Tuesday President Biden said United States would ban oil imports Russia,  before war produced  10 barrels oil world consumed, move further roiled markets.
" decision today  without cost here home. Putin' war already hurting American families gas pump," Mr. Biden said.
Administration officials,  often clashed domestic oil gas producers  Mr. Biden' climate change policies, took different tone Wednesday asked industry help. move came gasoline prices averaged $4.25 per gallon nationwide, highest levels since 2012 after adjusting inflation.
"We war footing, emergency,  responsibly increase short-term supply where  can right now stabilize market minimize harm American families," Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm told oil gas executives Wednesday industry conference Houston. " means you producing right now, where you can."
Ms. Granholm said ramping  oil gas production short term would  mean abandoning administration' goal moving away fossil fuels order fight global warming. Mr. Biden vowed slash America' greenhouse gas emissions least 50 percent below 2005 levels 2030.
"Yes, right now,  need oil gas production rise meet current demand," Ms. Granholm said. But, she added, "we're serious decarbonizing while providing reliable energy doesn' depend foreign adversaries."
Oil use industrialized countries dropped between 2005 2012, last time prices high,  consumption flatlined since, save dip during pandemic.
remains uncertain how much United States  actually do curb  dependence fossil fuels years ahead.  most recent annual outlook, U.. Energy Information Administration said oil gas expected remain nation' leading energy sources through 2050 without major shift policy. same  , scientists say, nations need largely eliminate fossil fuel emissions  want prevent most catastrophic effects global warming.
administration' main legislative proposal speeding  transition cleaner energy, Build Back Better Act, remains limbo. bill includes $555 billion spending deploy low-carbon technologies like wind, solar, geothermal nuclear power. Buyers electric vehicles would receive  $12,500 tax credits. bill  billions dollars make buildings energy efficient replace gas-powered furnaces electric versions.
theory, those measures could go long way toward shrinking America' reliance oil gas, though  would take time work. recent analysis think tank Energy Innovation estimated bill' electric vehicle provisions could cut U.. oil consumption 180 million barrels per  2030,  double what nation imported Russia last . Other provisions clean  power plants, buildings industry could cut U.. natural gas use 4.7 trillion cubic feet per  2030, equivalent 85 percent what Europe imported Russia last .
But legislation stalled evenly divided Senate. No Republicans support, Senator Joe Manchin III, key Democratic swing vote, said opposes current version.
Some oil gas executives Houston  week said while  acknowledge need tackle climate change, effort should take back seat urgent need increase fossil fuel production avoid economic disruption.
"Since consequences climate going 30 40 years down road, people going focus lot what happening now.  should," said Charif Souki, chairman Tellurian Inc, developer liquefied natural gas projects. "We can come back climate."
consensus among scientists dangers climate change already apparent now affecting corner planet, destructive storms fatal heat waves record drought.
same time, oil executives  conceded  may face limits how much additional oil gas  can produce short term. U.. oil production already nearly back prepandemic levels, just below 12 million barrels per day. Energy Information Administration predicted Tuesday output could rise 13 million barrels per day 2023.
"Nobody really anticipated needing grow significantly," Vicki Hollub, chief executive Occidental Petroleum said during panel discussion conference. "That' challenge now. you didn' plan growth, you're  going able achieve growth today."
Ms. Hollub said supply chain constraints hampering ability U.. oil gas producers rapidly increase production, labor shortages making difficult find qualified workers. same time, investors burned previous price crashes demanded companies focus returning cash shareholders instead investing growth.
Europe faces even bigger challenge. Since 2010, continent made major investments renewable energy efficiency measures helped reduce  consumption natural gas. But domestic production gas declined even faster,  means increasingly reliant exports places like Russia,  supplies nearly 40 percent gas Europeans use heat electricity.

Just decade ago, small, resource-rich nation Ecuador embarking bold transition hydroelectric power.
 many South American countries betting  abundant rivers, harnessed dams, could satisfy growing energy needs - help drive economic expansion, lifting millions poverty leading way  new era prosperity.
Today, those grand designs colliding warming climate.
Ecuador pummeled extraordinary drought, exacerbated global warming, engulfed much South America, drying rivers reservoirs putting country' power grid brink collapse.
Since September, daily energy cuts lasted long 14 hours. Highways turned inky black; entire neighborhoods lost running water, even internet cell service. One industry group says nation losing $12 million productivity sales hour power.
"My country adrift," said Gabriela Jijón, 46,  owns ice cream shop outside Quito, capital.
But Ecuador  alone. recent years, abnormally dry weather places around globe sent rivers extreme lows, draining hydropower resources places like Norway, Canada, Turkey even lush Costa Rica.
Zambia, highly dependent hydropower, faced daily power cuts  21 hours  . Parts China,  reliant water energy, suffered lengthy outages starting 2022.,   billion people live countries where  50 percent  energy comes hydroelectric plants, according Ember, global energy research institute. Yet, climate warms extreme weather events like drought become common - severe - many scientists expect hydropower become less reliable energy source.
 quarter hydroelectric dams places medium extreme risk water scarcity 2050, according 2022 study journal Water.
Ecuador, many ways, bellwether what  nations may face.
Some nations, including United States Ecuador' neighbors South America, Colombia Brazil, backup plans switch alternative sources, including fossil fuels, hydropower runs low.
But cost ensuring extra energy capacity prohibitive, many countries unprepared worsening conditions, said Nicolas Fulghum, senior analyst Ember.
China growing concern, Mr. Fulghum said. 2022 2023, dry conditions country' southwest prompted blackouts closed factories severely disrupted commerce.
country gets just 13 percent  power water - compared Ecuador' 70 percent -  because China  large  connected global economy, future droughts likely "cascading effects," Mr. Fulghum said.

2007, new leftist president, Rafael Correa, swept  office Ecuador, promising build modernized, socially environmentally conscious nation.
country  already suffered through monthslong energy crisis 1990s, then again 2009, both prompting blackouts caused drought.
Responding rising electricity demands growing population, hoping use electricity supercharge  vision nation, Mr. Correa' government invested billions dollars expand  energy output.
harnessing water,  burning oil gas, produce electricity,  new energy matrix supposed help mitigate effects global climate change -  make Ecuador victim.
Borrowing heavily Chinese lenders, Mr. Correa' government launched almost dozen new hydroelectric projects, massive dams became emblematic country' transformation.
One project particular, $2.2 billion dam known Coca Codo Sinclair, plagued design flaws, critics said, allegations some officials took bribes exchange awarding dam contract company Sinohydro.
Yet between 2007 2017, Mr. Correa left office, country' overall energy generation capacity rose 60 percent, according Ministry Energy Mines.
Other changes included new constitution eventually placed energy sector almost entirely under state control - something critics later said led mismanagement inefficiency.
Most growth came hydroelectricity, some increases fossil fuels, wind solar capacity.
Then, Mr. Correa left office, capacity generate electricity fell flat.

#21
Europe watching skies gremlin can roil  energy markets stoke political tensions between neighboring countries. This weather phenomenon, known Dunkelflaute, become source frustration government ministers potential pitfall  journey cleaner energy.
German term translates "dark doldrums," Dunkelflaute refers spate calm days dense clouds descend  northern Europe. This weather pattern may occur two 10 times , usually fall winter, lasts 24 hours longer.
past, these spells murky quiet would made little difference energy markets Europe. But recent years, countries like Germany Britain spent billions tackle climate change shifting cleaner sources energy, Dunkelflaute gained notoriety.
generation ago, Europe relied steady, predictable flows energy nuclear fossil fuel plants. Now, much region' power comes solar arrays wind farms, whose output varies whims weather.
Advocates wind solar power stress these technologies lower costs because  do  burn gas coal,   can contribute large price swings marketplace power.
During gloomy stillness Dunkelflaute, solar panels produce little power wind turbines slow halt. Without these two mainstays renewable energy, grid operators need call backup power plants like natural-gas-fired generators.
cost running these power plants higher,  drives  prices renewable sources falling behind, said Steve Moody, trading director Conrad Energy, power trading company Abingdon, England, supplies electricity grid through batteries  sources. "You  see high prices periods scarcity," said.
During stretch low wind mid-December, instance, British power prices briefly jumped 485 pounds per megawatt-hour, above average £70  last , according Drax Electric Insights, website tracks power.
Germany suffered particularly gloomy fall winter  , electricity production down  5 percent compared same time previous . Dunkelflaute mid-December caused electricity prices jump 14 times  average.
Because Dunkelflaute can blanket several countries same time,  effects can ripple through much northern Europe, putting pressure power systems. Dunkelflaute early November led price surges Denmark, Netherlands Belgium, Britain.
These weather systems often occur temperature cold, testing capacity power grid because affected countries may draw large volumes gas. Dunkelflaute,  can last several weeks, "can potential impact security supply," said official Britain' National Energy System Operator during industry webinar held  month discuss  effects.
growing use renewable energy can  lead times wind solar generation outpaces demand, leading extremely low prices - potential boon consumers ( problem investors power generation  may see lower profits  expected).
But attention lately focused downsides, specifically high power prices countries like Germany Denmark can spill   markets like Norway' Sweden', straining relationships between neighboring countries.
help ward off events like Dunkelflaute, European neighbors linked  power grids through network cables, often laid under North Baltic Seas. These conduits intended share power surpluses - like those times arise Norway' hydropower riches - address deficits. intent ensure sufficient supplies smoother prices.
Earlier  decade, instance, cable constructed bring Norwegian hydropower Britain during quiet wind spells, while sending surplus wind energy  direction.
web such cables now exists, energy experts say  needed.
But even strategy sharing power comes drawback. power-hungry country like Germany sucks large volumes electricity  Scandinavian neighbors,  interconnected system can drive  prices exporting countries, angering consumers.
"  no wind,  get high electricity prices  failed electricity system," Ebba Busch, Sweden' economy minister, wrote post social media.
Ms. Busch  blamed insufficient electricity resulting high prices Germany Berlin' decision shut off  nuclear reactors last , despite energy crunch country caused Russia cutting off natural gas supplies part  dispute West  war Ukraine.
Some power-exporting countries threatening turn protectionism buffer  citizens. Politicians Norway cast doubt continuing operate cables bring Norwegian hydropower Denmark, while Sweden postponed building second cable Germany. France, officials far-right National Rally party  proposed curbing electricity exports country' fleet nuclear power stations.
high recent prices "created  kind political backlash against cables," said Amund Vik, former Norwegian deputy energy minister now senior adviser Eurasia Group, political risk firm. Yet " European energy system needs integration secure, you do energy transition" added.
Analysts like Mr. Vik skeptical   major retreat interdependent European power market constructed  decades. But friction may complicate shift cleaner energy. several months winter ahead, next Dunkelflaute could right around corner.

WASHINGTON - While world leaders vowed scale back use fossil fuels help keep lid global warming, drastic upheaval markets oil, natural gas coal could complicate shift toward cleaner sources o energy.
Global oil prices soared  highest level seven years, nearing $90 per barrel, fears grow Russian invasion Ukraine. Europe grips severe natural gas crunch roiled energy markets worldwide. global demand coal, dirtiest fossil fuels, surged record highs economies bounce back pandemic lows.
There' broader lesson here, energy experts said. Even governments businesses invest low-carbon energy sources like wind solar power, world  remain deeply reliant fossil fuels years come. Unless transition carefully managed, many countries could face volatile energy prices  disruptions, turn, threaten undermine support policies reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
After coronavirus pandemic struck 2020, global investment oil gas projects declined 30 percent slow recover. But global demand oil snapped back faster projected reach records  , economies rebound. Supplies struggled keep .
top, recent geopolitical turmoil - including supply disruptions Kazakhstan fears Russian invasion Ukraine - lifted oil prices  highest levels since 2014.
Although Western oil companies drilling fewer wells since pandemic began, partly held back investors wary unprofitable projects, high prices could shift calculus. Tuesday, Exxon Mobil announced would increase spending new oil wells  projects  45 percent   after reporting $23 billion profits 2021,  best result seven years.

United States, rising gasoline prices - currently averaging $3.40 per gallon, dollar higher   ago - drag approval ratings President Biden,  struggling persuade Congress pass climate policies aimed reducing fossil-fuel emissions. same time, Biden administration defended moves issue new oil gas permits public lands, although those efforts slowed federal courts.
But high oil prices aren' always bad news clean energy. They can  depress oil demand, example, pushing people buy electric vehicles don' require gasoline. Last , electric cars made  20 percent new sales Europe 15 percent new sales China, according BloombergNEF, research group.
Gas Shortage Roils Europe
recent months, world struggled spiking prices natural gas, fuel used both power plants home heating, caused ripple effects across globe. Utility bills soared Italy South Korea, while fertilizer plants Britain Germany  curtail operations. (Natural gas key ingredient nitrogen-based fertilizer.)
causes gas crunch numerous: Global demand rebounded faster  supply since pandemic began; lower output hydropower dams China Brazil led surge gas imports; cold snap last spring across Europe increased demand reduced gas inventories.
crisis particularly acute Europe, where natural gas prices now five times high   ago. Officials racing procure new shipments gas overseas case Russia,  provides -third Europe' natural gas, curtails supplies event conflict  Ukraine.
There  signs gas crunch could undermine unity within European Union  policies fight climate change.
Officials currently debating sweeping new set clean-energy measures aimed cutting emissions 2030. Some nations, like Spain, called faster shift away fossil fuels reduce Europe' exposure gas markets. But others, like Poland, urged delay stricter climate action amid crisis.
there' possibility skyrocketing energy prices could bolster unrest akin "Yellow Vests" protests 2018,  forced French government backtrack plans increase fuel taxes way reduce emissions.
Across globe, rising natural gas prices provided boost coal,  typically produces twice much carbon dioxide gas burned electricity, driving  planet-warming emissions.
Global coal consumption reached record 2021 track rise further 2022, International Energy Agency recently said. partly because electricity demand surging countries like China India, investment renewable energy  kept pace. But high natural gas prices  spurred many electric utilities turn coal.
United States  example. Over past decade, advances fracking led boom domestic gas production, country become  world' largest exporters liquefied natural gas.
Those exports become key source global supply during latest crisis. But   boosted natural gas prices home,  turn means some utilities finding economical run  coal plants often. Last , U.. coal power emissions increased 17 percent after years falling steadily, putting country further off course reaching  climate goals.
" really illustrates how much we've depended cheap natural gas prices keep coal decline," said Kate Larsen, partner Rhodium Group, research firm. "Overall,  still expect coal decline further years ahead,  unless  new policies put place clean  power sector, coal industry could see bit lifeline  big swings gas market."
Bumpy Transition
recent essay, Fatih Birol, executive director International Energy Agency, argued climate change policies  blame current global energy crisis. But, warned, " does  mean road net zero emissions  smooth."

"This needs happen quickly," wrote, " global energy markets  face turbulent volatile period ahead."

Demand electricity around world rising faster  expected, making harder countries slash  emissions keep global warming check, International Energy Agency said Wednesday.
Over next decade, world poised add equivalent Japan' annual electricity demand grids each , driven surging power needs new factories, electric vehicles, air-conditioners data centers, according agency' annual World Energy Outlook, comprehensive report global energy trends., agency now expects global electricity demand 6 percent higher 2035  forecast last .
It'  dire news climate change: report concludes countries likely build enough low-emissions power plants end decade - primarily solar, wind nuclear power - match rise demand. Rapid growth renewable energy should least prevent global emissions rising drastically could cause coal, oil natural gas use peak  decade.
But stop global warming, many countries pledged reduce  emissions zero around midcentury. goal slipping reach: Countries would need build low-carbon electricity sources twice fast they're currently doing between now 2035 meet  climate targets.
" energy history, we've witnessed age coal age oil," said Fatih Birol, executive director International Energy Agency. "We're now moving speed  age electricity,   define global energy system going forward."
Electricity demand historically gone  societies grow affluent. But few recent trends accelerated growth, report said.
Rising temperatures, fueled global warming, spurring people buy air-conditioners run them often. India, air-conditioning sales doubled after series brutal heat waves  .
Electric vehicles  becoming popular: While sales growth slowed   United States Europe, it' different story China. end  , half new cars sold China projected electric, roughly 60 percent plug- vehicles sold  now cheaper   gasoline-powered counterparts.
Tech companies  building power-hungry data centers frenetic pace, driven interest artificial intelligence. While data centers account just 1 percent global electricity demand,  often concentrated clusters can strain local grids.
extra demand makes tougher tackle climate change.
Previously, agency expected global consumption coal, dirtiest fossil fuels, would drop significantly 2030 wind solar power expanded. But electricity demand rising quickly places like China India, coal use now projected decline gradually. Some utilities United States extending life coal-fired power plants  headed retirement.
Global carbon-dioxide emissions expected fall just 3 percent 2030 under policies nations currently pursuing, agency said. Emissions need fall 33 percent  decade meet ambitious climate goals governments agreed United Nations climate talks.
" higher energy use, even fast renewables growth doesn' translate fast falls carbon-dioxide emissions," said Dave Jones, global insight director Ember, energy think tank.
Speeding  production clean energy difficult many countries. China,  accounted 60 percent growth wind solar power last , electric grids need significant upgrades handle fluctuations renewable output. Europe, sales electric heat pumps slowed sharply first half   gas prices eased. United States, lack transmission lines hindering wind projects.
Big changes fossil-fuel markets  looming. Growing electric vehicle sales could cause global oil demand plateau  decade, agency said. United States Qatar currently building  many terminals export liquefied natural gas world could see "oversupply" gas later  decade, said. could push down global energy prices, offering relief countries faced painful price spikes after Russia' invasion Ukraine.

Countries representing half global energy demand holding elections 2024. includes United States, where former President Donald J. Trump promised repeal federal policies favoring renewable power electric cars encourage oil gas drilling returns office.
result, report said, " near-term uncertainty  usual"  how global energy policies  evolve.

#27
Make beef-free meals. Compost food scraps. Replace natural gas stove electric .
These fairly simple ways people can help combat climate change  own kitchens. Still, most Americans don' do. Why?
Because means changing lifelong habits. Because  believe won' make difference. Because  think  friends neighbors aren' doing.
Research shows  easy motivate people curb  emissions,  some strategies do work, experts trying identify best ones.
Magnus Bergquist, associate professor psychology University Gothenburg Sweden, said widespread behavior transformation difficult, people often contradictory goals. example, changing habits buying energy-efficient products, "can conflict people' goals seeking comfort, saving money gaining social acceptance," said video interview.
Even those best intentions can run  against such conflicts. Leah Murphy, 63, New Paltz, N.Y., said she recycles, reduced her use plastic, shops canvas bags installed energy-efficient lightbulbs, among  efforts.
Nonetheless, " 30 years ago, my first child born, arranged diaper service because supposed better environment," Ms. Murphy said interview. Yet  still lot diapers wash, after six weeks, she switched disposable diapers.
" told myself energy used wash cloth diapers probably just bad environment disposables," she said. " experience represents start long series rationalizations between convenience conscience -- often  , convenience prevailing."
same time,  concern promoting personal solutions address global climate change lets corporations governments off hook even plays   hands. example, carbon footprint calculator created oil gas company BP 2004 part advertising campaign help people measure  impact environment. Critics said simply way shift responsibility big companies everyday consumers.
But it' wrong look solutions vast issue climate change solely good bad, said Anthony Leiserowitz, director Yale Program Climate Change Communication,  researches public' knowledge, attitudes, behavior policy preferences climate change.
BP did create calculator " way put onus individual consumers,  it' still valuable basic concept say, ', you really do want reduce your own emissions, shouldn' you probably start estimate how much you're emitting?'" said video interview. "Both can true."
According organization Project Drawdown,  advances climate solutions, individual household actions taken together - reducing food waste installing LED lighting - potential produce 25 percent 30 percent reductions greenhouse emissions needed avoid extremely dangerous aspects climate change.
Take eating beef. Cows, lesser extent goats sheep, significant contributors greenhouse gases through methane  emit gas manure. cows' pastures typically created cutting down forests,  releases carbon dioxide stored trees.
But according World Resource Institute, research organization, each person living high beef-consuming countries - like United States - ate 1.5 fewer burgers week, need agricultural expansion deforestation would eliminated greenhouse gases significantly reduced.
Still, modifying even such seemingly minor behavior difficult. Since 1970s, overall beef consumption U.. dropped considerably, because climate, health animal welfare concerns. But needs decrease substantially address climate change. Instead, slightly increased 2022 highest  decade.
It'  clear why,  it' example how hard induce change.
"Some people naïve idea  just educate people, change  follow," Professor Bergquist said. "But doesn' take much understand  know  should exercise;  know  should eat healthy;   don' do. So, knowledge necessary  insufficient factor. top,  need motivation."
coauthored analysis data 430 primary studies strategies improve environmental-related behavior, such recycling biking walking instead driving.
six interventions change people' behaviors, study found, providing data information least successful, while financial incentives like rebates, coupons fines can make difference.
But research  found social comparisons - what my friends neighbors doing? -  biggest effect: customers told how  utility use compared  neighbors', higher users often decreased  consumption  two percent. solar panels, people persuaded install them  see them  neighbors' rooftops.
Mr. Leiserowitz said witnessed  own neighborhood. "We first put solar panels roof 10 years ago, now  dozens solar panels roof," said. "It'  because went talking people - it' because people can see somebody else who' much like them adopted  new technology."
But collective changes, reducing littering wearing seatbelts drinking less alcohol, often evolve gradually. took many years numerous initiatives reduce smoking, example. included education dangers, restrictions smoking public areas, prohibitive taxes cigarettes, public stigma greater access programs helped people quit.
"Changes slow hard see -  you look backward you see things seemed outlandish increasingly commonplace," said Jason Mark, editor chief Sierra Magazine, video interview.
2019 report Rare, 50--old global nonprofit uses behavioral insights encourage action protect environment, examined seven personal choices  impact climate change: switching electric vehicle, reducing air travel, eating plant-rich diet, offsetting carbon, reducing food waste, tending carbon-sequestering soil purchasing green energy.

Make beef-free meals. Compost food scraps. Replace natural gas stove electric .
These fairly simple ways people can help combat climate change  own kitchens. Still, most Americans don' do. Why?
Because means changing lifelong habits. Because  believe won' make difference. Because  think  friends neighbors aren' doing.
Research shows  easy motivate people curb  emissions,  some strategies do work, experts trying identify best ones.
Magnus Bergquist, associate professor psychology University Gothenburg Sweden, said widespread behavior transformation difficult, people often contradictory goals. example, changing habits buying energy-efficient products, "can conflict people' goals seeking comfort, saving money gaining social acceptance," said video interview.
Even those best intentions can run  against such conflicts. Leah Murphy, 63, New Paltz, N.Y., said she recycles, reduced her use plastic, shops canvas bags installed energy-efficient lightbulbs, among  efforts.
Nonetheless, " 30 years ago, my first child born, arranged diaper service because supposed better environment," Ms. Murphy said interview. Yet  still lot diapers wash, after six weeks, she switched disposable diapers.
" told myself energy used wash cloth diapers probably just bad environment disposables," she said. " experience represents start long series rationalizations between convenience conscience -- often  , convenience prevailing."
same time,  concern promoting personal solutions address global climate change lets corporations governments off hook even plays   hands. example, carbon footprint calculator created oil gas company BP 2004 part advertising campaign help people measure  impact environment. Let' say you live typical American household. doesn' exist,  any sense except data set,  it' easy enough imagine. Maybe it' your aunt', your neighbor', bit like your own. task shrinking our societal footprint most urgent problem our era - perhaps most intractable. most experts, first steps obvious yield largest least invasive cuts. Since electricity makes  25 percent United States' five billion  tons yearly emissions,  likely begins decarbonizing grid. Next comes push electrify transportation sector regulate industrial production; each contributes 27 24 percent emissions, respectively. Then come several smaller cuts, buildings  live appliances  use, policies already having success Europe Canada: replacing gas-burning furnaces electric heat pumps, updating building efficiencies banning air-conditioners fridges use hydrofluorocarbon. Exactly how much these cuts reduce our footprints difficult say, because our country spans entire continent several climates. Late last , traveled Uruguay hope glimpsing  possibility. Wedged between  larger routinely travelogued neighbors, Brazil Argentina, small Latin American country exists something anomaly. carbon footprint hovering around global median 4.5 tons per capita, falls within narrow tier nearly developed countries within sight two tons per capita - estimated amount needed limit world 1.5 degrees Celsius warming. Something Uruguay' character can read you descend toward. One smallest least densely populated countries Earth, composed almost entirely single sweep grasslands,  unfolds gently practically uninterrupted cities landmarks. Its highest point, Cerro Catedral, reaches 1,685 feet. Its ratio cattle people: 4 1. Even  official name, República Oriental del Uruguay, Republic East River Uruguay, seems modest commentary  relationship Argentina, whose capital, Buenos Aires, lies just across water Montevideo, like fun-house reflection busier metropolis. sky full low scudding clouds landed early December. Montevideo, ocean breeze ran down boulevards,  lined eucalyptus trees worn Art Deco apartment buildings. After  restrictions, cafes stood open busy, rich already leaving  summer homes Punta del Este. Along Río de la Plata,  becomes Atlantic Ocean east city, people walked what  call La Rambla, separated beach crumbling brick sea wall.
Cerro neighborhood, west downtown, sat beneath painting jaguar living room Marí Esther Francia. Francia thin 71 wore matching set patterned pants blouse, her dark hair held back loosely oversize glasses. former activist health care worker, she  intimate observer Uruguay' past, curious hear what she made  future. Her paintings - mostly landscapes animals - hung everywhere her small apartment. They suggested undercurrents. emerald prairie extended across brilliant horizon, while spectral figures toiled beneath mudlike caves. Francia told me she did  know what future Uruguay headed toward, only  building upon remains  history.
Francia grew  Salto, Argentine border, before moving Montevideo 1960s. time, Uruguay prosperous  embattled, fledgling social democracy growing  unequal Marxist-Leninist group called Tupamaros  begun robbing banks distribute money poor. "What earned working  enough eat," Francia said. "  many people much worse condition." Uruguay' relatively small size concentration ( half  3.5 million people live Montevideo)  long provided country collective sense purpose - literacy close 95 percent health care coverage universal right -   brought  inequalities rapid boil. One Tupamaros' famous slogans read: "Everybody dances nobody dances."
1969, just three months after Francia married, her husband, Alfredo Cultelli, killed -called Taking Pando,  Tupamaros seized commercial hub. Four years later, while serving prison sentence, Francia learned military  dissolved Parliament. Suspending right vote, ruling junta adopted neoliberal economic agenda inspired Chicago school began conducting widespread terror campaign. twin policies  broke tiny country. Industrial productivity initially shot , military cut tariffs social entitlements. But  growth came price. writer Eduardo Galeano once put : " Uruguay, people prison  prices could free." 1980, somewhere between 300,000 400,000  exiled estimated  500 imprisoned - highest percentage political incarceration world - many them subjected torture, including Francia.
Then 1982, bottom fell economy - peso crumbled economy shrank 16 percent two years. Francia returned political asylum Sweden 1985, she found her once-prosperous country unrecognizable: streets  empty residents joked "last person country  turned off lights." much next couple decades, unemployment poverty fluctuated wildly, country struggled free itself collapsing economies Brazil Argentina weight  own past.
2009, Uruguay elected unlikely leader - José Mujica. former baker' assistant flower merchant, Mujica  become notorious  guerrilla leaders Tupamaros, whom staged least  bank robbery, before being shot arrested 1970. spent 13 years prison, escaping least twice, suffering torture long stretches solitary confinement bottom. After  election, Mujica' image populist folk hero only further burnished  deep commitment social welfare simplicity. Forgoing presidential palace,  opened homeless, chose continue living  chrysanthemum farm, donating 90 percent  salary charity driving  1987 Volkswagen Beetle Parliament. Today he' considered many Uruguay throughout world archetypal Uruguayan.
president, Mujica inherited disastrous energy crisis - makings extraordinary revolution. Since 1940s, Uruguay' power  come mix hydroelectric dams oil-fired thermal plants,  country grew throughout 1990s 2000s, delayed plans developing new energy sources became increasingly reliant  thermal plants,  yoking country' economy unstable commodity prices. Blackouts fuel shortages spread. 2008, Mujica' predecessor, Tabaré Vázquez, tapped physics professor named Ramón Méndez national director energy. Méndez saw problem existential. While blackouts threatened near-term economy, continued reliance oil undermined country' sovereignty. question occupied  work: How could country achieve energy independence,  just now   future?
Many Uruguayans thought answer nuclear energy,  Méndez looked question, felt nuclear just same: stopgap. "You're still producing waste taken away human life thousands years," told me. After manufacturing costs renewables dropped within competitive range fossil fuels 2009,  appeal quickly became apparent: Because costs fixed - just investment upkeep - country could unshackle  energy sector commodities. "Energy  just  thing," Méndez said. "You look basis energy system - physical aspects, social aspects, geopolitical aspects." Uruguay' landscape offered additional advantage: abundance bright windy ranchland suitable large installations. September 2008, just five months after assuming office, Méndez submitted plan countrywide transition renewable power.
plan ambitious: proposed decarbonizing grid 2020, decreasing country' energy consumption 20 percent creating domestic green-energy sector scratch. passed cabinet,  Mujica took office 2010, suggested new approach. Despite  party holding majority, believed  needed reach agreement opposition. asked Mujica why, told me, "Governments pass people remain." plan required attracting international investment building infrastructure next 25 years, broad political support only way ensure  long-term stability. some Uruguayans found Mujica' words naïve, many others found  actions inspiring. Méndez, , praised "shared national narrative" Mujica developed. Much Baethgen' thinking rooted  boyhood. grew  Montevideo  spent summers  sister' 2,400-acre ranch, riding horses learning grassland environments. family grazed  cattle grass, rather  hay grain, rotating native herds through wild prairies. what gauchos  always done. Baethgen came United States graduate school, earned  doctorate 1987 spent next two decades consulting around world, mostly farmers ranchers developing world. developed countries, watched ranching industrialize  force deforestation.  half crops grown United States now used feed livestock,  grow fat feedlots cleared prairies once supported millions bison. job brought Baethgen back Uruguay around 2000, struck how little   come home. Ranchers Uruguay didn' use antibiotics hormones, 90 percent cattle still fed hundreds species native grasses, symbiotic relationship helped sequester carbon ground.
Baethgen thought many answers sustainable cattle industry lay within land itself. explained grasslands function lot like forests: grasses pull carbon air, converting  plant matter through photosynthesis. cows graze, bacteria  stomachs help break fiber down, metabolic process builds protein-dense muscle   methane byproduct,  gets burped. This cycle becomes problematic, Baethgen said, only these ecological processes fall balance. " grasslands overgrazed," said, " soil becomes degraded, doesn' absorb store much carbon." same true undergrazing. fields grow thick woody stalks, cows simply avoid eating them regenerative cycle breaks down.
One reason global cattle industry  become  damaging, Baethgen said, too many grasslands  razed degraded. short term, feedlots produced food, often lower emissions, since cows got fatter faster burped less frequently,   long term, without grasslands recycle carbon, net emissions built . Baethgen' perspective, damaged field thus represented huge opportunity: restoring grasslands, could  only pull greenhouse gases  ground,   grow beef. since 1990s, Uruguay managed remarkable feat: increasing  annual production beef without any increase greenhouse gases - doing  natural pasturelands.

Aug. 16, several months after returned home, President Biden signed most significant climate legislation ever passed, Inflation Reduction Act,  included package aimed lowering carbon emissions transportation energy sectors. Independent modeling Rhodium Group, research firm New York, estimated bill would cut emissions between 32 42 percent end 2030, compared 2005. Once mainly theoretical math problem, cutting nearly  half American footprint now seemed conceivable.

Even Wyoming, where coal still dominates, alternative sources power made steady gains.
Fossil fuels still generate majority America' electricity,  shift coal natural gas renewable power helped reduce planet-warming carbon dioxide emissions  harmful pollution.
Last , coal top electricity fuel 10 states, down 32 states 2001. Natural gas largely took  during time,  wind  emerged leading power source across Midwest.
Still, experts say  long way go country wants zero emissions power sector fight climate change, goal set President Biden.
Switching coal gas "gets you part way ," said Melissa Lott, professor Climate School Columbia University, because burning natural gas power produces fewer carbon dioxide emissions  burning coal. But fewer emissions  same zero emissions, she added. "Many technologies, including renewables, need built quickly get us way our climate goals," Dr. Lott said.
Mr. Biden' signature climate energy law, 2022 Inflation Reduction Act, aimed turbocharge growth renewable wind solar energy nationwide support  technologies could reduce emissions power sector, like nuclear energy, advanced batteries carbon capture storage gas plants. But future law remains uncertain election , Republicans promising repeal many  clean-energy provisions.
What happens federal level only part equation. States power accelerate, slow down block new energy development, too.

#40
first time since nations began meeting three decades ago confront climate change, diplomats nearly 200 countries approved global pact explicitly calls "transitioning away fossil fuels" like oil, gas coal dangerously heating planet.
sweeping agreement,  comes during hottest  recorded history, reached Wednesday after two weeks furious debate United Nations climate summit Dubai. European leaders many nations most vulnerable climate-fueled disasters urging language called complete "phaseout" fossil fuels. But proposal faced intense pushback major oil exporters like Saudi Arabia Iraq, fast-growing countries like India Nigeria.
end, negotiators struck compromise: new deal calls countries accelerate global shift away fossil fuels  decade "just, orderly equitable manner," quit adding carbon dioxide atmosphere entirely midcentury.  calls nations triple amount renewable energy, like wind solar power, installed around world 2030 slash emissions methane, greenhouse gas potent  carbon dioxide short term.
While past U.N. climate deals urged countries reduce emissions,  shied away explicitly mentioning words "fossil fuels," even though burning oil, gas coal primary cause global warming.
"Humanity finally done what long, long, long overdue," said Wopke Hoekstra, European commissioner climate action. "Thirty years - 30 years! -  spent arrive beginning end fossil fuels."
new deal  legally binding can',  own, force any country act. Yet many politicians, environmentalists business leaders gathered Dubai hoped would send message investors policymakers shift away fossil fuels unstoppable. Over next two years, each nation supposed submit detailed, formal plan how intends curb greenhouse gas emissions through 2035. Wednesday' agreement meant guide those plans.
"This  transition  happen  day ," Susana Muhamad, Colombia' environmental minister, said  week. "Whole economies societies dependent fossil fuels. Fossil capital   disappear just because  made decision here." But, she added, agreement sends " strong political message  pathway."
deal represents diplomatic victory United Arab Emirates, oil-rich nation hosted these talks glittering, sprawling expo center Dubai under smoggy skies just 11 miles away largest natural gas power plant world.
Sultan Al Jaber, Emirati official oil executive presiding  talks, faced complaints conflicts interest weathered early calls  removal. record number fossil fuel lobbyists flooded summit. Abu Dhabi National Oil Company, company run Mr. Al Jaber, investing least $150 billion  next five years increase drilling.
But Mr. Al Jaber  called phaseout fossil fuels "inevitable" staked  reputation being able persuade  oil-producing nations sign major new climate agreement.
"Through night early hours morning  worked collectively consensus," Mr. Al Jaber said Wednesday morning before room full applauding negotiators. " promised would roll  my sleeves. We basis make transformational change happen."
remains seen countries  follow through agreement. Scientists say nations would need slash  greenhouse gas emissions roughly 43 percent  decade  hope limit total global warming 1.5 degrees Celsius, 2.7 degrees Fahrenheit, compared preindustrial levels. Beyond level, scientists say, humans could struggle adapt rising seas, wildfires, extreme storms drought.
Yet global fossil-fuel emissions soared record highs  , nations currently track cut pollution less  10 percent  decade world already heated  1.2 degrees Celsius. Many scientists say now highly unlikely humanity can limit warming 1.5 degrees, though  add countries should still do everything  can keep warming low possible.
Representatives small islands, whose coasts disappearing under rising seas whose wells filling saltwater, said new climate agreement  "litany loopholes"  enough avert catastrophe.
"This process failed us," said Anne Rasmussen, lead negotiator Samoa,  complained deal  approved while group 39 small island nations  room. " course correction needed  secured."
Past climate agreements often failed encourage meaningful action. 2021, nations struck deal Glasgow "phase down" coal-fired power plants. But Britain approved new coal mine just   later global coal use since soared record highs.
Even negotiators United States Europe pressed forcefully deal reduce fossil fuel use, environmentalists pointed oil production United States surging, while European countries spending billions new terminals import liquefied natural gas amid war Ukraine.
American officials talked  fact Congress  recently approved hundreds billions dollars adopt manufacture clean energy technologies like solar panels, electric vehicles heat pumps would help curb world' appetite oil, coal natural gas.
bleary-eyed diplomats Dubai argued -night sessions  language text,  forced wrestle realities stark challenges global transition away fossil fuels greater detail  ever before.
Saudi Arabia oil gas companies argued talks should focus emissions, instead fossil fuels themselves, saying technologies such carbon capture storage could trap bury greenhouse gases oil gas allow  continued use. date, nations struggled deploy technology broad scale.
Other world leaders countered best way cut emissions switch cleaner forms energy like solar, wind, nuclear, reserving carbon capture rare situations where alternatives unavailable.
final text calls nations accelerate carbon capture "particularly hard--abate sectors." But some negotiators expressed concern fossil-fuel companies could seize language continue emitting high rates while promising capture emissions later.
Some oil producers already see wiggle room deal. television interview after summit, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, Saudi energy minister, said agreement "buried issue immediately phasing phasing down" fossil fuels instead "left space countries choose  own way."  insisted Saudi Arabia' oil exports would  affected.
final agreement  language recognizing -called transitional fuels can play role transition clean energy ensuring energy security. "Transitional fuels" widely seen code natural gas, something gas-producing countries like Russia Iran  called. Some nations seeking end fossil fuels lamented inclusion language.

earlier draft agreement  urged nations stop issuing permits new coal-fired power plants unless  could capture bury  carbon dioxide emissions. But countries like China India,  still building large new coal plants satisfy growing energy demand, opposed overly tight restrictions. language new coal plants removed final version.
Many African countries sharply criticized idea countries should reduce  fossil fuel use same pace. Without outside financial help,  argued, African nations would need exploit  own oil gas reserves order grow rich enough fund clean energy transition.
"Asking Nigeria, indeed, asking Africa, phase fossil fuels like asking us stop breathing without life support," said Ishaq Salako, Nigeria' environmental minister. "  acceptable  possible."
Some world leaders criticized wealthy emitters like United States, Europe Japan failing provide enough financial support low-income countries help them transition away fossil fuels. places like Africa, Latin America Southeast Asia, developing nations facing soaring interest rates made difficult finance new renewable energy projects.
new agreement nods importance finance,  countries agreed tackle issue next round climate talks Baku, Azerbaijan, next .

Heat pumps,  both warm cool buildings powered electricity, touted answer curbing greenhouse gas emissions produced homes, businesses office buildings,  responsible -third emissions New York State.
But how do  work? How much do  cost? New York ready them? can  really help solve climate crisis? Here some heat pump basics.
Why heat pumps better environment?
Currently,  mostly burn fossil fuels produce heat. This causes pollution. Heat pumps -electric.
Even though most electricity still comes combustion, United States slowly transitioning renewable power like hydro, wind solar.  shift occurs, heat pumps  help eliminate greenhouse gases.
How do  work?
heat pump moves heat.
consists boxy component outside sleek-looking blower inside. thermostat controls temperature. During warm weather, pump works just like air-conditioner rerouting indoor heat outdoors.
it' cold outside, process reversed: Heat chilly outdoor air extracted delivered indoors help refrigerants compressor.
Will heat pumps overburden electricity grid?
New York City' transition renewable energy electrification happening gradually, involving many projects moving parts. Con Edison making investments grid prepare increase demand, said Jen Hensley, senior vice president company. But time being, grid ready heat pumps, she added.
devices highly efficient,  should help limit growing burden grid, said Rohit T. Aggarwala, city' climate chief.
Miguel Modestino, director sustainable engineering initiative Tandon School Engineering New York University, explained pumps' efficiency: They move heat,  don' create. Using same amount electricity, heat pump can provide three four times warmth  plug- space heater.
   kind heat pump?
Yes. Air-source heat pumps most common. Geothermal pumps, another kind, take heat below ground, where temperature stable, Dr. Modestino said.
Geothermal systems,  tend expensive install, can provide energy large buildings even entire communities. But New York City, where real estate underground jammed pipes, cables subway tunnels, geothermal pumps, few notable exceptions, can difficult install. So air-source pumps work better city.
Heat pump installations rise New York City, Con Edison customers completing  30,000 them since 2020. Credit...Jackie Molloy New York Times
What kinds buildings can use pumps?
Small buildings (- four-family homes some businesses) ideal.
New constructions using larger-scale heat pumps,  can placed roofs across entire floors, said Greg Elcock, vice president energy efficiency Con Ed.
It' older, larger buildings problem, said. "We call them hard--electrify stock."
Installing heat pump pipes across  12 stories building still major challenge, said Pallavi Mantha, associate Arup, global sustainable-development company.
large buildings worst polluters. How can  reach New York' climate goals?
City law states properties larger  25,000 square feet -   50,000 - must curb  emissions 40 percent 2030.
state program helping several city properties, like Empire State Building, develop heat recovery systems partial electrification plans. But " still technical advances happening  don' answers yet," Ms. Mantha said.
Until then, buildings can take incremental approach replacing windows, improving insulation, reducing consumption, Ms. Mantha said.
" you do , creates enabling path electrification," she said. "It' buying time until technology policy evolve."
people really buying heat pumps?
They starting. New York City, Con Ed customers completed  30,000 installations since 2020. across state, nearly 23,000 heat pump projects installed 2022, threefold increase  before.
United States, shipments heat pumps outpaced those gas furnaces  15,000 units January, according Air-Conditioning, Heating Refrigeration Institute, trade association manufacturers.
