Aren'  expensive?
Yes. Installing heat pump single-family home can cost upward $20,000.
There financing options, however. Through 2032, federal tax credit  $2,000 available installation, New York State' Clean Heat program offers rebates through utility companies can amount between $8,000 $12,000 savings, according Con Ed spokesman. There  financial assistance available specifically low- middle-income households. Mr. Rosenthal said  electricity bills 30 percent less summer, spring fall.
Later  , Inflation Reduction Act expected release funding New Yorkers, state officials said.
pain install ?
Like any home project, experience can vary. "It' major surgery," said Mr. Rosenthal,  compared weekslong construction renovation. " surgical," said Robert Montalvo, homeowner Bronx, whose pump system installed  day.
level disruption duration project depends space piping work involved, said Victor Rodriguez, whose Brooklyn company, Ice Age Mechanical, installs heat pumps small buildings homes.

While Inflation Reduction Act, signed  law President Biden 2022, largest-ever federal law aimed addressing climate change, did  set any emissions standards define national strategy addressing climate change.
Instead, 1990 reauthorization Clean Air Act, bipartisan effort signed  law first President George Bush, last time Congress established guidelines any number pollution regulations, leaving federal guidelines behind times.
" created opportunities presidents, especially you shift control executive branch shift parties, make fairly substantial changes fly," said Barry Rabe, professor public policy University Michigan  studied executive actions environmental issues.
During Trump' first term, used executive actions undo much President Barack Obama' climate work. President Biden then followed suit, using executive actions reinstate some Obama policies.
This tit--tat exchange left United States scattershot approach climate policy during decade effects global warming become increasingly clear.
" hard build enduring durable set policies through executive action," Rabe said. "But quite possible reverse delay actions taken your immediate predecessor."

What scientists learned extreme marine heat wave California
They call " blob."
decade ago, sea surface temperatures Pacific shot  11 degrees Fahrenheit hotter  normal. high pressure system parked  ocean, winds churn  cold, nutrient-rich water depths died down. Stagnant, warm water spread across Northeast Pacific marine heat wave lasted three years.
Under surface, food web broke down ecosystems convulsed. Seabirds washed  shore. carcasses knee-deep places, impossible miss.
Researchers still untangling threads what happened,  caution against drawing universal conclusions single regional event. But blob fundamentally changed many scientists' understanding what climate change could do life ocean. disaster  our richest sources information what happens marine life temperature rises. - Delger Erdenesanaa

One way reduce solar' damage wildlife-friendly fencing.

hurdle now way, least  kind laser-instigated fusion. makes easier envision future fusion power plants would produce essentially no planet-warming carbon dioxide  greenhouse gases. experiment like  Livermore,   tiny pellet vaporized 192 laser beams,  thing,  power plant rapidly vaporizes thousands upon thousands pellets safely draws off released energy conversion  electricity quite another. That'  say can' done,  just take time. Lots time.
same goes  major approach developing fusion,  uses huge, powerful electromagnets confine cloud hydrogen gas stripped  electrons, raising temperatures point where fusion can occur. This -called tokamak approach (taken Russian acronym doughnut-like chamber contains gas cloud).
2017, visited largest tokamak project, ITER, southern France. It' mind-bogglingly complex machine, multinational effort being assembled parts produced many countries. project first envisioned 1980s; hope  produce fusion mid-2030s.
But ITER, like Livermore project, only experiment. Even ITER works, designing building plant captures energy tokamak converts electricity most likely very far-off.
world needs sharply cut emissions soon. limit warming 1.5 degrees Celsius above 19th-century levels, stricter two limits came 2015 Paris climate talks, emissions need reach nearly zero 2050.

could power start flow?

"I'm very, very worried , because insurance markets  opaque weak link," Dr. Sastry said. "There parallels some complex linkages happened 2008, where  weak unregulated market spills  banking system."
Regulators worry failing understand those ripple effects could  just put single bank trouble  even become contagion would undermine financial system. They set  systems monitor potential problems,  some financial reformers criticized inadequate.
But while European Central Bank made climate risk consideration  policy oversight, Federal Reserve resisted taking active role, despite indications extreme weather feeding inflation high interest rates slowing transition clean energy.
" argument, 'Unless  can convincingly show it' part our mandate, Congress should deal, it' none our business,'" said Johannes Stroebel, finance professor New York University' Stern School Business.

report Intergovernmental Panel Climate Change, body experts convened United Nations, warns unless countries drastically accelerate efforts  next few years slash  emissions coal, oil natural gas, goal limiting global warming 1.5 degrees Celsius, 2.7 degrees Fahrenheit,  likely reach end  decade.

Scientists say global warming  largely halt once humans stop adding heat-trapping gases atmosphere, concept known "net zero" emissions.
new report contains glimmers optimism. Over past decade, many nations adopted ambitious climate policies, scaled back plans new coal plants used subsidies regulations expand renewable energy. agency created 1920 Federal Power Commission control hydropower dams. President Franklin D. Roosevelt later championed legislation dismantle utility monopolies, 1935 Congress passed Federal Power Act, requiring commission set "just reasonable" wholesale electricity prices.
1970s, became Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, FERC, got expanded mandate regulate interstate electricity, hydroelectric projects oil gas pipelines.
" transmission pipeline infrastructure entire country runs through   agency," said Rob Gramlich, president Grid Strategies, power sector consulting firm. "Most people  involved FERC happy stays news," added.
recent years, commission become focal point connection between power grid climate change. Utilities burn gas coal produce electricity second largest source pollution driving global warming.
2021 Richard Glick,   appointed chair commission President Biden, said agency should fully consider impacts climate change approving natural gas pipelines.
focus climate change, came controversy.
Senator Joe Manchin, West Virginia Democrat champion coal gas, irate Mr. Glick saying climate change should consideration during pipeline discussions. chairman Senate Energy Natural Resources Commission, Mr. Manchin refused hold confirmation hearing Mr. Glick,  ultimately stepped down.
His departure left commission 2-2 partisan split. Under FERC' rules, no  three  five commissioners may single party.
hear Mr. Schumer tell, that' Democratic strategizing went  high gear.
Efforts hammer bipartisan legislation speed  electricity transmission remain limbo. Democrats want speed  pace adding solar wind grid while Republicans return want faster easier permitting fossil fuel projects pipelines.

" Republican Party used party led transmission policy," said. But now "it' seen facilitating Biden achieving  climate agenda, they're against just against Biden."

Spend enough time talking climate change, you'll eventually get pulled toward  question: What would happen global warming strike most people urgent crisis?
There many candidates: extreme heat domes hitting major American cities, coupled widespread blackouts. Extended droughts across America' farmland, top decades-long depletion groundwater, leading food shortages. two-foot rise sea levels caused collapse Thwaites glacier Antarctica, putting entire coastal regions United States underwater.
Those scenarios seem long way off many Americans (even  might  ). But what thing  make climate change feel like crisis most everyone already underway across country? What it' erosion something entirely mundane, even little boring,   essential modern life?
My colleague Mira Rojanasakul spent most   digging  how climate shocks hitting America' home insurance industry. What we've found looks awful lot like early stages crisis.
climate-fueled disasters get worse, home insurance becoming money-losing business country. Without insurance, you can' get mortgage; without mortgage, most Americans can' buy home.
Last , insurers lost money homeowners coverage 18 states,  12 states five years ago eight states 2013. Nationally,  past decade, insurers paid claims   received premiums,, since 2018,  1.9 million home insurance contracts nationwide dropped.
result what you could call Iowa effect: Even formerly low-risk places like state, insurers raising premiums, restricting coverage simply leaving.
used impossible know  communities around country insurers pulling back.
This week, changed. Senate Budget Committee issued findings yearlong investigation, showing where insurers dropping customers -  , county county. result new way map growing threats extreme weather.
map county-level nonrenewal rates United States. Light yellow counties parts Minnesota, New York Pennsylvania signify lower rates, while orange dark red counties illustrate very high nonrenewal rates parts Carolinas, Gulf Coast, Midwest California.
" climate crisis coming our way  just polar bears, it'  just green jobs," Senator Sheldon Whitehouse, Democrat Rhode Island chairman Senate Budget Committee, said during hearing Wednesday investigation' findings. " actually coming through your mail slot, form insurance cancellations, insurance nonrenewals dramatic increases insurance costs."
sure, climate change isn' only thing driving insurers' decisions. State regulation, rising cost construction, fraud litigation, even changes global reinsurance market play role.
But unlike those  factors, climate change doesn' respond quickly policy changes, nor cyclical. Disasters going keep getting worse.
Our reporting points three things watch next:
* What  happen home prices? Fewer prospective buyers means home values likely fall areas where insurance becomes especially expensive hard get. But pace those changes hard predict.
* How  state governments respond? Insurance regulated states, some  experimenting new ways halt erosion  markets. Those interventions tend revolve around giving concessions insurers stick around, including making easier charge higher premiums. It' far clear, though, those steps  enough.
* Will insurance complicate Donald Trump' plan ignore climate change? Treasury Department under President Biden laid groundwork gather data climate change' effects insurance market, then backed off face pressure states insurers, my colleague Emily Flitter reported  . But incoming Trump administration could pressured intervene, especially since some highest premium increases states carried November.
"Desperation clarifies mind," Mr. Whitehouse told me  week. "Once looming danger true, systemic, 2008-style mortgage economic meltdown nears, then it' time everybody pay attention."
fix: Vermont   passed nation' first climate Superfund law,   require large fossil fuel companies pay climate-change driven damages caused  emissions  three-decade period.
State lawmakers New York passed similar bill  , several  states considered similar measures.
Vermont supposed submit report State Legislature Jan. 15 detailing how feasible would implement law. money fossil fuel companies would pay would go toward climate adaptation efforts.
law intended recover costs incurred state, opposed being punitive fossil fuel companies, said Julie Moore, Vermont secretary natural resources, noting state local governments prepare climate disruption. "This approach try secure resources necessary adaptation," Moore said.
New York State, both chambers Statehouse negotiating Gov. Kathy Hochul' office possible amendments bill, according spokesperson  bill' sponsors. Hochul, Democrat, until end  act legislation.
spokesperson governor' office declined comment bill.
obstacles: fossil fuel industry yet bring any legal challenges against Vermont  new law,  officials climate activists expect sector . "It' question, ," Moore said.
Neither American Petroleum Institute nor Vermont Petroleum Association responded requests comment whether  intend bring legal action.

One biggest issues related climate change Vermont faces flooding,  lot work scientists looking cost climate change focused heat, Moore said.
" first state gate,  want make sure  put forward best case possible," she said.

#64
One biggest obstacles expanding clean energy United States lack power lines. Building new transmission lines can take decade because permitting delays local opposition. But  may faster, cheaper solution, according two reports released Tuesday.
Replacing existing power lines cables made state---art materials could roughly double capacity electric grid many parts country, making room much wind solar power.
This technique, known "advanced reconductoring," widely used  countries. But many U.. utilities slow embrace because  unfamiliarity technology regulatory bureaucratic hurdles, researchers found.
"We pretty astonished how big increase capacity you can get reconductoring," said Amol Phadke, senior scientist University California, Berkeley,  contributed  reports released Tuesday. Working GridLab, consulting firm, researchers Berkeley looked what would happen advanced reconductoring broadly adopted.
"It'  only thing  need do upgrade grid,  can major part solution," Dr. Phadke said.
Today, most power lines consist steel cores surrounded strands aluminum, design that' around century. 2000s, several companies developed cables used smaller, lighter cores such carbon fiber could hold aluminum. These advanced cables can carry  twice much current older models.
Replacing old lines can done relatively quickly. 2011, AEP, utility Texas, urgently needed deliver power Lower Rio Grande Valley meet soaring population growth. would taken too long acquire land permits build towers new transmission line. Instead, AEP replaced 240 miles wires existing line advanced conductors,  took less  three years increased carrying capacity lines 40 percent.
many places, upgrading power lines advanced conductors could nearly double capacity existing transmission corridors less  half cost building new lines, researchers found. utilities began deploying advanced conductors nationwide scale - replacing thousands miles wires -  could add four times much transmission capacity 2035  currently pace do.
would allow use much solar wind power thousands projects proposed  can' move forward because local grids too clogged accommodate them.

Installing advanced conductors promising idea,  questions remain, including how much additional wind solar power can built near existing lines, said Shinjini Menon, vice president asset management wildfire safety Southern California Edison,  nation' largest utilities. Power companies would probably still need build lots new lines reach remote windy sunny areas, she said.
"We agree advanced conductors going very, very useful," said Ms. Menon, whose company already embarked multiple reconductoring projects California. "But how far can  take? jury' still."
Experts broadly agree sluggish build- electric grid Achilles' heel transition cleaner energy. Energy Department estimates nation' network transmission lines may need expand two-thirds 2035 meet President Biden' goals power country clean energy.
But building transmission lines become brutal slog, can take decade developers site new line through multiple counties, receive permission patchwork different agencies address lawsuits spoiled views damage ecosystems. Last , United States added just 251 miles high-voltage transmission lines, number declining decade.
climate stakes high. 2022, Congress approved hundreds billions dollars solar panels, wind turbines, electric vehicles  nonpolluting technologies tackle global warming part Inflation Reduction Act. But United States can' add new transmission capacity quickly, roughly half emission reductions expected law may  materialize, researchers Princeton-led REPEAT Project found.
difficulty building new lines led many energy experts industry officials explore ways squeeze existing grid. includes "grid-enhancing technologies" such sensors allow utilities send power through existing lines without overloading them advanced controls allow operators ease congestion grid. Studies found these techniques can increase grid capacity 10 30 percent low cost.
Countries like Belgium Netherlands widely deploying advanced conductors order integrate wind solar power, said Emilia Chojkiewicz,  authors Berkeley report.
"We talked transmission system planners    said  no-brainer," Ms. Chojkiewicz said. "It' often difficult get new rights way lines, reconductoring much faster."
reconductoring  effective, why don' utilities United States do? question focus second report released Tuesday, GridLab Energy Innovation, nonprofit organization.
One problem fragmented nature America' electricity system,  actually three grids run 3,200 different utilities complex patchwork regional planners regulators. means new technologies -  require careful study worker retraining - sometimes spread slowly   do countries just handful grid operators.
"Many utilities risk averse," said Dave Bryant, chief technology officer CTC Global, leading manufacturer advanced conductors projects  60 countries.
There  mismatched incentives, report found. Because way  utilities compensated,  often financial incentive build new lines rather  upgrade existing equipment. Conversely, some regulators wary higher upfront cost advanced conductors - even  pay themselves  long run. Many utilities  little motivation cooperate  another long-term transmission planning.
" biggest barrier industry regulators still caught short-term, reactive mind-set," said Casey Baker, senior program manager GridLab. "But now we're era where  need grid grow very quickly, our existing processes haven' caught  reality."
may starting change some places. Montana, Northwestern Energy recently replaced part aging line advanced conductors reduce wildfire risk - new line sagged less heat, making less likely make contact trees. Pleased results, Montana legislators passed bill would give utilities financial incentives install advanced conductors. bill Virginia would require utilities consider technology.
electricity demand beginning surge first time two decades because new data centers, factories electric vehicles, creating bottlenecks grid, many utilities getting   wariness new technologies.
"We're seeing lot interest grid-enhancing technologies, whether it' reconductoring  options," said Pedro Pizarro, president chief executive Edison International, California power company, chairman Edison Electric Institute, utility trade organization. "There' sense urgency."

Since 2007, power outages become  common Eskom, national electricity supplier, devised schedule cut power different neighborhoods different times. calls these periods national frustration "loadshedding."
Eskom' troubles result century poor management, experts energy economics said interviews. During apartheid, utility,  mostly supplied country' white minority, subsidized cost electricity big industries like mining, meaning many lucrative companies did  pay  fair share.
legacy low tariffs continues hamper Eskom' ability cover basic costs like maintenance, said Jesse Burton, researcher Energy Systems Research Group University Cape Town..N.C. came power 1994, failed expand utility time fleet began buckle under growing demand shrinking revenue. Corruption incompetence construction new power plants only made things worse. Mismanagement successive presidential administrations hobbled Eskom until collapsed.
"It' institutional failure level," said Ms. Burton. "They're debt spiral, they're maintenance spiral."
Since taking office five years ago, Mr. Ramaphosa created broad-reaching strategy save Eskom. included plan fix existing power stations, introduce renewable energy allow private companies produce power.
But plan produced few visible results. Those charged solving problem bitterly divided. Gwede Mantashe, South Africa' energy minister  Mr. Ramaphosa' top lieutenants.N.C., accused Andre de Ruyter, chief executive Eskom, December trying undermine.N.C.-led government.

fatal flaw Mr. Ramaphosa' effort rescue Eskom tried do too many things once, said Khaya Sithole, economic analyst Johannesburg. His plan, like those previous administrations, failed focus fundamental challenge: maintenance.
"Maintenance does  amount new project,  it'  new project, you're  going P.R. exercise where you're cutting ribbon," Mr. Sithole said.

" people   suffer most acutely these problems poor marginalized, that' where seeds social revolution going planted," said Mr. Sithole.

Turning rivers oceans  giant sponges carbon dioxide
problem: keep climate change tolerable levels, countries  probably do  just slash emissions - they'll  need remove hundreds billions tons carbon dioxide atmosphere  century.
fix: Nova Scotia, startup called CarbonRun grinding  limestone sprinkling  rivers poisoned acid rain. Not only does  technique help local salmon populations,  helps pull greenhouse gases sky.
Typically, rivers contain carbon dioxide constantly escaping  air, where traps heat warms planet. But adding limestone converts some carbon dioxide  stable molecule instead stays underwater washes  sea, where should remain trapped thousands years.
potential: There hundreds acidified rivers worldwide, CarbonRun estimates  process, known river liming, could remove hundreds millions tons carbon each .
might just start. similar techniques could done ocean - adding alkaline substance like limestone magnesium oxide seawater, example - humanity could potentially remove billions tons each .
So far, oceans tougher work. It' tricky prove adding alkalinity actually removes carbon turbulent high seas, early research efforts faced public backlash. Some scientists hope CarbonRun can pioneer, because it' easier verify carbon removal happens rivers, river liming highly popular local communities, since can help revive devastated fisheries.
obstacles: CarbonRun needs cut costs,  means figuring how inexpensively mine transport limestone. company needs two tons rock ton carbon removes.
company  needs buyers. Right now, only handful wealthy tech companies, like Microsoft Stripe, willing pay startups remove carbon air. Some experts say carbon removal should considered public good governments should pay,  it' still early days.
One policy twist: Congress offers generous tax credit - worth $180 per ton - companies remove carbon air using complex direct air capture machines,  subsidy does  cover techniques involve oceans rivers.
What' next: CarbonRun received $25 million remove initial 55,442 tons carbon dioxide atmosphere starting scale . Elsewhere, researchers Halifax Massachusetts studying whether alkalinity enhancement can work oceans. - Brad Plumer

Mexico world' 11th-largest oil producer. gripped deadly heat wave. Now, it' elected  president woman rare pedigree: left--center climate scientist doctorate energy engineering named Claudia Sheinbaum.
Ms. Sheinbaum no stranger politics nor environmental crises. She mayor Mexico City, vibrant metropolitan area 23 million faces dire water crisis. She helped write Intergovernmental Panel Climate Change reports, sweeping United Nations documents warned world hazards burning fossil fuels.
Ms. Sheinbaum  balance numerous, sometimes contradictory, tests she takes office. Federal budgets tight. Energy demands rising. Mexico' national oil company heavily indebted. She'll face challenges poverty, migration, organized crime relations next president United States.
would folly predict what she  do,  it' worth looking what she said done energy environmental issues  far her career.
First, her record.
mayor Mexico City, she began electrifying city' public bus fleet. She set  huge rooftop solar array city' main wholesale market. She expanded bike lanes, making permanent several kilometers pandemic-era pop- paths.
She criticized environmentalists backing  country' most controversial infrastructure projects, 1,500-kilometer -called Maya Train corridor,  cuts across forests archaeological sites connect tourist sites like Cancún rural areas Yucatán Peninsula.
Mexico' energy sector, Ms. Sheinbaum said campaign trail she wanted expand renewable energy infrastructure, unlike her predecessor, André Manuel López Obrador. But she  said she would continue support Mexican state-owned oil company, Petróleos Mexicanos, Pemex, keep under state control.
Pemex produces just under 2 million barrels oil day. Ms. Sheinbaum said she would maintain those levels, while  expanding company' mission include lithium production. Lithium key component electric batteries pivotal global transition cleaner energy.
Mr. López Obrador limited private investments renewable energy projects, including United States, Ms. Sheinbaum continue policy, could significantly slow down country' clean energy transition.
"Claudia environmental scientist unlike her mentor, AMLO, believes decarbonization boosting renewables," said Shannon O'Neil, Mexico specialist Council Foreign Relations, referring Mr. López Obrador  initials. "But she  statist, wanting Mexico' energy transition led controlled cash-strapped, state-owned enterprises."

Case Wind Power
Wind power can incredibly useful cutting greenhouse gas emissions rapidly heating planet, experts say.
may surprise some: After, wind doesn' always blow it' needed, average turbine only runs maximum power -third time.

United States largest importer Canadian fossil fuels, most coming oil sands Alberta,  require large amounts energy production.
Canada' oil gas industry country' largest source carbon emissions, accounting -third overall total.

Canadian government imposed taxes carbon helped reduce emissions many sectors economy 1990 2022. Still, Canada' overall greenhouse emissions rose 16.5 percent during period, largely because oil gas industry raised  production levels.
" significant moment Canada," Julia Levin, associate director climate Environmental Defence, environmental group, said before announcement. "But  troublesome loopholes." These include allowing companies fail make cuts buy offsets, she said.

#81
Just decade ago, small, resource-rich nation Ecuador embarking bold transition hydroelectric power.
 many South American countries betting  abundant rivers, harnessed dams, could satisfy growing energy needs - help drive economic expansion, lifting millions poverty leading way  new era prosperity.
Today, those grand designs colliding warming climate.
Ecuador pummeled extraordinary drought, exacerbated global warming, engulfed much South America, drying rivers reservoirs putting country' power grid brink collapse.
Since September, daily energy cuts lasted long 14 hours. Highways turned inky black; entire neighborhoods lost running water, even internet cell service. One industry group says nation losing $12 million productivity sales hour power.
"My country adrift," said Gabriela Jijón, 46,  owns ice cream shop outside Quito, capital.
But Ecuador  alone. recent years, abnormally dry weather places around globe sent rivers extreme lows, draining hydropower resources places like Norway, Canada, Turkey even lush Costa Rica.
Zambia, highly dependent hydropower, faced daily power cuts  21 hours  . Parts China,  reliant water energy, suffered lengthy outages starting 2022.,   billion people live countries where  50 percent  energy comes hydroelectric plants, according Ember, global energy research institute. Yet, climate warms extreme weather events like drought become common - severe - many scientists expect hydropower become less reliable energy source.
 quarter hydroelectric dams places medium extreme risk water scarcity 2050, according 2022 study journal Water.
Ecuador, many ways, bellwether what  nations may face.
Some nations, including United States Ecuador' neighbors South America, Colombia Brazil, backup plans switch alternative sources, including fossil fuels, hydropower runs low.
But cost ensuring extra energy capacity prohibitive, many countries unprepared worsening conditions, said Nicolas Fulghum, senior analyst Ember.
stop lights long stretches, dozens Venezuelan migrants living Ecuador now direct traffic, informal job earns them few coins passers-. Abel Oviedo, 30,  them.
China growing concern, Mr. Fulghum said. 2022 2023, dry conditions country' southwest prompted blackouts closed factories severely disrupted commerce.
country gets just 13 percent  power water - compared Ecuador' 70 percent -  because China  large  connected global economy, future droughts likely "cascading effects," Mr. Fulghum said.
Ecuador, President Daniel Noboa, facing re-election February, pledged recently end power cuts  month, claiming energy country purchasing nearby Colombia,  factors, would ease power crisis.
But energy experts expect any relief temporary. Unless  deluge - two weeks heavy rain needed raise reservoir levels - regular energy outages could endure until least 2026, said Iván Endara, professor Coastal Higher Polytechnic School, Ecuadorean university.
truly end crisis, said, years work must put  diversifying building country' energy sector.
Promise Hydropower
2007, new leftist president, Rafael Correa, swept  office Ecuador, promising build modernized, socially environmentally conscious nation.
country  already suffered through monthslong energy crisis 1990s, then again 2009, both prompting blackouts caused drought.
Responding rising electricity demands growing population, hoping use electricity supercharge  vision nation, Mr. Correa' government invested billions dollars expand  energy output.
harnessing water,  burning oil gas, produce electricity,  new energy matrix supposed help mitigate effects global climate change -  make Ecuador victim.
Borrowing heavily Chinese lenders, Mr. Correa' government launched almost dozen new hydroelectric projects, massive dams became emblematic country' transformation.
ne project particular, $2.2 billion dam known Coca Codo Sinclair, plagued design flaws, critics said, allegations some officials took bribes exchange awarding dam contract company Sinohydro.
Yet between 2007 2017, Mr. Correa left office, country' overall energy generation capacity rose 60 percent, according Ministry Energy Mines.
Other changes included new constitution eventually placed energy sector almost entirely under state control - something critics later said led mismanagement inefficiency.
Most growth came hydroelectricity, some increases fossil fuels, wind solar capacity.
Then, Mr. Correa left office, capacity generate electricity fell flat.
former president, interview  current home Belgium, said climate change " problem" root Ecuador' energy crisis.
Instead, blamed three governments followed  failing maintain hydro thermoelectric power plants, faulted them  building  energy sources, leading decreased capacity inability keep producing electricity adverse conditions.
" never seen such rapid destruction country times peace," said.
(Mr. Correa left Ecuador 2017, convicted 2020 corruption charges unrelated energy projects.)
interviews, two three presidents  succeeded Mr. Correa - Lenin Moreno Guillermo Lasso - said  did invest expanding capacity, noting energy projects often take years come online. But both noted  faced economic headwinds Mr. Correa did  - first, falling oil prices greatly contracted country' economy,  highly dependent petroleum exports.
Then came pandemic. Mr. Lasso described  challenge : "Do buy vaccines do invest thermoelectric plants?"
But Mr. Moreno,  took office 2017, said "principal" problem country' "excessive focus hydroelectric energy"  "left system extremely vulnerable climate change phenomena."
Mr. Lasso,  took office 2021, cited something else: called Ecuador' political culture main driver crisis, describing shortsighted prone neglecting long-term solutions major challenges - including energy security.
Representatives current president, Mr. Noboa, declined comment.

Who Claudia Sheinbaum?
trained scientist Ph.D. energy engineering, Ms. Sheinbaum (pronounced SHANE-balm) spent years straddling academia politics, where her career closely tracked Mr. López Obrador' rise. Her first foray  government environment minister Mexico City Mr. López Obrador became mayor 2000.
Years later, she participated United Nations panel climate scientists awarded Nobel Peace Prize. Mr. López Obrador became president, she won office mayor Mexico City,  largest metropolises hemisphere.
Ms. Sheinbaum, 62, describes herself "obsessive" "disciplined." She known exacting boss little patience laziness, someone  can squeeze last drop her staff.
descendant Ashkenazi Sephardic Jews  emigrated Mexico 20th century, Ms. Sheinbaum  country' first Jewish president - watershed moment some trivial detail others  rarely seen her discuss her heritage.

times, she pulled away  policies. During pandemic, she tested aggressively while trusted good-luck charms; fighting crime, she invested intelligence police while relied military.

recent weeks, violence between warring factions Sinaloa Cartel caused spike deaths kidnappings northwest Mexico, bringing people' lives standstill cities such Culiacán.

PHOLA, South Africa - Dozens cattle farmers swarmed excavator clawing  green pasture South Africa' coal belt, shouting threat man behind controls: Stop digging,  would topple machine.

Mr. Mathibela  fellow farmers many ways unlikely protesters coal. Many them work industry - Mr. Mathibela' primary income comes transporting coal. Their investments cattle relatively new, hedge against expectation South Africa cannot run coal forever.
But global climate summit gets underway Sharm el Sheikh, Egypt, South Africa proving prime example how hard  countries dependent coal make transition cleaner, renewable energy sources.
80 percent South Africa' electricity comes coal. South Africa, most industrialized diversified economy continent, pledged phase. last year' global climate summit, Scotland, wealthy nations pledged $8.5 billion help South Africa shift energy systems produce less pollution.
But most coal mining ventures, once run white-owned conglomerates, shifted Black ownership under laws passed after apartheid governing party, African National Congress. wealth finally Black hands, political environmental analysts concerned.N.C.  union allies may loath let go industry - especially urging Europeans,  profited generations South Africa' resources.

"It' counter things we've talking terms South Africa' commitment reduce emissions," said Nokwanda Maseko, senior economist Trade & Industrial Policy Strategies, economic research institution South Africa.
President Cyril Ramaphosa publicly promoted shift renewables,   minister mineral resources, Gwede Mantashe, maintained coal  remain strong part country' energy mix years come.

"Our continent only contributed 1 percent damage that' done climate,  believe industrialized countries developed need live  commitment  made," said.
raised concerns most funding pledged  country, group includes Britain, France, Germany United States, slated come loans.  only add debt country can ill afford, said, arguing country needs grants instead loans, meaningful transition  require $98 billion  five years.

August, height Egyptian summer, government  ordered government offices, stadiums, hotels malls turn down air-conditioning turn off lights conserve power. natural gas savings would sold Europe hefty markup, helping Egypt weather economic hurricane Europe survive energy crisis brought Russia' invasion Ukraine.

government Cairo, however, Europe sudden need natural gas very good thing.

Even Egypt' two L.N.G. plants running full capacity,  could export only 11 percent what Europe used get Russia - assuming Europe can afford drop Egyptian gas, majority  goes highest global bidder.
But plants operated below capacity, pointing deeper issue: Despite windfall Mediterranean gas field, Egypt barely any surplus gas  own sell.
Domestic demand power soared nearly 35 percent since 2015, driven rapidly growing population, while gas production dropped steadily since last fall, thanks part overdrilling.
close gap free gas sell, Egypt fueling some  power plants mazut, low-quality fuel oil burns dirtier shortens plants' life spans, said Mohamed El Sobki, energy engineering professor Cairo University. Thanks mazut, Egypt' electricity sector begun emitting carbon dioxide steeply higher rates.
"This double-edged sword, really," Professor el-Sobki said. "We increasing economic prospects natural gas,  , we're hurting environment."

PARIS - France said Wednesday would renationalize  state-backed electricity giant help ensure country' energy sovereignty Europe faces worsening energy crisis Russia' war Ukraine.
move would give government control fix swirling storm problems plagued France' nuclear energy program, biggest Europe, time President Emmanuel Macron pledged blunt pain increasing living costs shielding consumers soaring energy prices.
Élisabeth Borne, French prime minister, told lawmakers Wednesday her first major speech before Parliament shift needed ensure France' energy independence while  meeting major goal combating climate change.
" energy transition requires nuclear power," she said.
Though France gets 70 percent  electricity nuclear power, bigger share  any  country world, Ms. Borne said could  no longer count Russian oil gas.
government must ensure  energy sovereignty holding 100 percent capital company, Électricité de France, EDF, she said,  84 percent currently. company France' main electricity producer operates  nuclear plants.
Economic interventionism government strong tradition France, even mostly moved away sweeping nationalizations 1980s under François Mitterrand, Socialist president time.
Still, step symbolic  President Emmanuel Macron. former investment banker,  elected 2017 avowedly pro-business platform promised cut regulation reduce government spending. But did  take long him follow  predecessors' footsteps.
2017,  government nationalized France' largest shipyard, STX France, prevent Italian competitor taking . recently, Covid-19 pandemic conflict between Russia Ukraine accelerated  pivot free-market reformer state intervention advocate.
Mr. Macron now insistent government should champion economic energy sovereignty bolster France' independence meet climate goals, including regaining control key national industries.
France less dependent  European neighbors like Germany Russian gas oil. But maintain relative independence, upgrading country' aging nuclear reactors become crucial government war Ukraine sent energy prices soaring, fueling inflation making cost living  biggest concerns French people.
February, Mr. Macron announced 51.7 billion euro blueprint overhaul France' nuclear program included plans EDF construct first  14 mammoth next-generation pressurized water reactors 2035.
Élie Cohen, economist  studied nuclear sector, said " only solution nationalization" because " government chosen energy mix centered nuclear power, because EDF now compelled build reactors while doesn' resources do ."
government  already hinted considering renationalization. During  campaign re-election, Mr. Macron  said news conference wanted implement long-term energy plans would entail "regaining capital control several industrial players."
EDF  France' most prominent industrial giants. Last , company employed  165,000 people earned revenue 85 billion euros, $86 billion.
But most France' nuclear infrastructure built 1980s suffered lack investment came head recent months mix unexpected maintenance issues shut down around half country' atomic reactors - most Europe - sent France' nuclear output tumbling  lowest level nearly 30 years.

decision placed European Union' heavy thumb scale global debate how how quickly major industrialized economies can move  heavy reliance fossil fuels - immediately proved controversial, prompting boos opponents inside outside parliamentary building Strasbourg, France.
Critics said would lock prolong Europe' reliance fossil fuels, while measure' proponents, including European Commission, E.U. executive arm drafted, said part pragmatic approach transition renewable energy, especially Europe seeks wean itself off Russian fuel imports wake invasion Ukraine.
measure  works even before Russia' tanks crossed border,  gained urgency European Union responded invasion banning Russian energy sources, including most coal oil. Left untouched Russian gas,  Europe remains heavily dependent.
Russian invasion presented European countries urgent choice: get gas anywhere   Russia, double down renewable sources like wind solar.
Wednesday' vote signaled Europe' intention prolong  reliance gas - principal component  methane,  speeds  global warming.
choice could undermine competing European imperative cut  greenhouse gas emissions  half 2030. E3G, energy research group, said " odds overall direction E.U. taking."
amendment Parliament backed Wednesday part broader E.U. effort give banks  organizations tools evaluate  projects deserve loans funds basis being environmentally friendly.
policy, known "taxonomy," meant stop "greenwashing," pervasive practice mislabeling energy projects environmentally friendly, could determine where hundreds billions dollars investments  flow coming decades.

new classification gas likely make far harder meet climate goal championed last international climate negotiations: cutting methane.

short, cutting methane emissions would slow down warming quickly,  argument European Union itself used joined United States last November announcing global pledge reduce methane emissions 30 percent 2030.

French plan aimed cementing country' position Europe' biggest atomic power producer positioning Électricité de France, EDF, troubled state-backed operator, compete aggressively against Chinese American companies growing global market nuclear energy.
estimated starting price 50 billion euros ($57 billion), Mr. Macron' blueprint consists constructing six mammoth next-generation pressurized water reactors existing nuclear sites around France starting 2028, option consider building  eight 2050.
Saying  "fallen behind" nuclear energy race, Mr. Macron said France would  build prototype small modular reactor - new type scaled-down nuclear power plant - 2030, pitting country against growing number others pushing technology.

U.. Backs Effort Lower Carbon Output Developing Countries

'Tired Digging'

Statues greet motorists main roundabout mining hub Congo' Copperbelt. One depicts industrial miner hard hat, headlamp boots; another shoeless, shirtless man ragged shorts holding pickax. They tell story country' dual mining economies: industrial artisanal.
High-tech, industrial mines run global corporations like China Molybdenum employ thousands people Congo' cobalt sector, while   own problems,  largely  responsible country' tarnished reputation abroad.
It' different story artisanal sector, where Mr. Yuma plans focus bulk  stated reforms. Consisting ordinary adults no formal training, sometimes even children, artisanal mining mostly unregulated often involves trespassers scavenging land owned industrial mines. Along main highway bisecting many mines, steady streams diggers motorbikes loaded down bags looted cobalt - each worth $175 - dodge checkpoints popping sunflower thickets.
Unable find  jobs, thousands parents send  children search cobalt. recent morning, group young boys hunched  road running through two industrial mines, collecting rocks  dropped off large trucks.
work  children dangerous - makeshift mines where some died after climbing dozens feet  earth through narrow tunnels prone collapse.
Kasulo, where Mr. Yuma showcasing  plans, illustrates gold-rush-like fervor can trigger dangerous mining practices. mine, authorized Gécamines, nothing  series crude gashes size city blocks carved  earth.
Once thriving rural village, Kasulo became mining strip after resident uncovered chunks cobalt underneath home. discovery set off frenzy, hundreds people digging   yards.
Today, mango tree few purple bougainvillea bushes, leftovers residents' gardens, only remnants village life. Orange tarps tied down frayed ropes block rainwater flooding hand-dug shafts where workers lower themselves chip rock extract chunks cobalt.
Georges Punga regular mine. Now 41, Mr. Punga said started working diamond mines 11. Ever since, traveled country searching Congo' unrivaled storehouse treasures underfoot: first gold, then copper,, past three years, cobalt.
Mr. Punga paused  digging  afternoon tugged  dusty blue trousers away  sneakers. Scars crisscrossed  shins years injuries job. earns less  $10 day - just enough, said, support  family keep  children school instead sending them mines.
" could find another job, I'd do," said. "I'm tired digging."

KISANFU, Democratic Republic Congo - Just  red dirt road, across expanse tall, dew-soaked weeds, bulldozers hollowing yawning new canyon central world' urgent race against global warming.
 decade, vast expanse untouched land controlled American company. Now Chinese mining conglomerate bought, racing retrieve  buried treasure: millions tons cobalt.
73, Kyahile Mangi lived here long enough predict path ahead. Once blasting starts, walls mud-brick homes  crack. Chemicals  seep  river where women do laundry dishes while worrying hippo attacks. Soon manager mine  announce everyone needs relocated.
"We know our ground rich," said Mr. Mangi, village chief   knows residents  share little mine' wealth.
This wooded stretch southeast Democratic Republic Congo, called Kisanfu, holds  largest purest untapped reserves cobalt world.
gray metal, typically extracted copper deposits, historically secondary interest miners. But demand set explode worldwide because used electric-car batteries, helping them run longer without charge.
Outsiders discovering - exploiting - natural resources  impoverished Central African country following tired colonial-era pattern. United States turned Congo uranium help build bombs dropped Hiroshima Nagasaki then spent decades, billions dollars, seeking protect  mining interests here.
Now,  two-thirds world' cobalt production coming Congo, country once again taking center stage major automakers commit battling climate change transitioning gasoline-burning vehicles battery-powered ones. new automobiles rely host minerals metals often  abundant United States oil-rich Middle East,  sustained last energy era.
But quest Congo' cobalt demonstrated how clean energy revolution, meant save planet perilously warming temperatures age enlightened self-interest, caught familiar cycle exploitation, greed gamesmanship often puts narrow national aspirations above else, investigation New York Times found.
Times dispatched reporters across three continents drawn  competition cobalt, relatively obscure raw material along lithium, nickel graphite gained exceptional value world trying set fossil fuels aside.
 100 interviews thousands pages documents show race cobalt set off power struggle Congo, storehouse these increasingly prized resources, lured foreigners intent dominating next epoch global energy.
particular, rivalry between China United States could far-reaching implications shared goal safeguarding earth. least here Congo, China  far winning contest, both Obama Trump administrations having stood idly company backed Chinese government bought two country' largest cobalt deposits  past five years.
significance those purchases becomes clearer, China United States entered new "Great Game" sorts. LONDON - Britain prepares host landmark climate summit Glasgow  week, milestones  own evolution climate-friendly economy vivid display along railroad line London Scotland.
Near Gainsborough, river town 150 miles north capital,  Britain' last coal-fired power plants still spews carbon dioxide  gases  air. Another 150 miles north, off coast seaside port Blyth, slender blades five turbines offshore wind farm turn lazily breeze.
two plants, both owned French utility giant EDF, illustrate how far Britain come. coal station, restarted recently cover shortfall electricity, slated taken operation next , while company plans install experimental floating turbines waters off Blyth.
"We're talking huge transition," said Paul Spence, director strategy corporate affairs EDF, referring Britain' goal being carbon-neutral economy 2050. " lot things need happen keep lights."
Britain  just host climate meeting, known COP26, credible claim being global leader climate policy. birthplace Industrial Revolution, Britain became first country legally mandate reductions greenhouse-gas emissions through Climate Change Act 2008. Its high-tech windmills superannuated smokestacks only most visible evidence three-decade campaign.
Having built world' largest offshore wind industry, Britain reduced emissions 44 percent 1990 levels. Its target cut them least 68 percent 2030  most ambitious any major economy, according Climate Action Tracker, scientific analysis policies countries.
Britain achieves target,  far clear, would  handful countries doing enough fulfill key goal Paris Agreement: limiting long-term rise planet' temperatures 1.5 degrees Celsius.
hit  headline number, Prime Minister Boris Johnson' government set series attention-grabbing goals: End sale gasoline- diesel-fueled cars 2030; end use coal gas-fired power plants 2035; end sale fossil-fueled home heating systems 2035.
" U.K. first blocks climate act, inspired Sweden then Germany," said Johan Rockström, director Potsdam Institute Climate Impact Research Germany. " U.K. successfully able phase coal,  very symbolic because first started England."

2006, British government adviser, Nicholas Stern, produced seminal study economic effects climate change,  framed debate before 2009 summit Copenhagen set stage Climate Act, passed under Labour prime minister, Gordon Brown.

"We basically ran E.U. climate policy," Mr. Burke said.

Wind turbines generated electricity  coal-burning power plants across United States March April, outstripping dirtiest fuel two consecutive months first time, according Energy Information Administration.
crossover wind coal generation latest milestone country' energy transition renewables rise coal declines. recent years, breakthroughs technology lowered cost building new wind turbines, solar panels battery storage, helping renewable energy replace coal cheapest power source many places.
Renewable energy  gotten boost tax credits awarded under Inflation Reduction Act, climate law Congress passed 2022. states passed regulations mandating utilities transition away fossil fuels, particularly coal.  20 states - including Minnesota, North Carolina Nebraska - enacted legislation requires utilities get  electricity clean, carbon-free sources 2050 before.
Those three factors - shifts economics energy, federal tax credits state mandates - led explosive growth renewable energy recent years, said Timothy Fox, managing director ClearView Energy Partners, consulting firm.
" project developers considering  resources deploy grid,  look 20, 30, 40 years down line," Mr. Fox said. " perspective, it' hard envision building coal plant today."
Environmentalists  focused economics coal-fired power plants recent years, strategy contributed closing coal plants New England. Michigan Louisiana, climate groups successfully persuaded state regulators stop utilities recouping  losses burning coal passing costs customers.
Coal plants retired rapid pace  past 25 years, Energy Information Administration data shows. 2000 2024, total coal capacity United States nearly halved, while wind capacity increased  60 times. Natural gas capacity nearly tripled during time started replace coal around 2005, fracking boom made large quantities cheap natural gas available.
April 2024, fossil fuels still accounted majority electricity generation United States, natural gas generating 39 percent power coal generating 12 percent. Nuclear power followed fossil fuels, supplying 18.5 percent electricity. Wind generated 15 percent while solar responsible 6 percent.
Analysts expect wind energy  grow provide around 35 percent electricity needs United States Canada 2050, said Cory Arce Gessert, vice president project development DNV, energy consulting firm.
"Even five years ago, you could see wind little supplement energy system," Ms. Gessert said. " now  seeing major component, necessary component."
United States, wind produces most electricity spring least summer.
April consistently month where coal generation falls below renewable energy some coal plant operators turn off  generators because electricity demand tends drop spring. Coal use typically rises winter summer months power demand spikes heating air-conditioning.

Nations COP29 Climate Summit Agree Annual $300 Billion Pledge
Delegates developing countries described deal' financial target, $300 billion per  2035, lacking ambition needed help  governments combat climate change.
" amount proposed mobilized abysmally poor. It' paltry sum.  something  enable conducive climate action. I'm sorry say  cannot accept." "Should remind us what convention says? noted developed countries  largest share historical current global emissions.  walk back home $300 [billion dollars]  say developed countries taking lead,  insult what convention says."
Negotiators  year' United Nations climate summit struck agreement early Sunday Baku, Azerbaijan, triple flow money help developing countries adopt cleaner energy cope effects climate change. Under deal, wealthy nations pledged reach $300 billion per  support 2035,  current target $100 billion.
Independent experts, however, placed needs developing countries much higher, $1.3 trillion per . amount  say must invested energy transitions lower-income countries, addition what those countries already spend, keep planet' average temperature rise under 1.5 degrees Celsius. Beyond threshold, scientists say, global warming  become dangerous harder reverse.
deal struck annual U.N.-sponsored climate talks calls private companies international lenders like World Bank cover hundreds billions shortfall. seen some kind escape clause rich countries.
soon Azerbaijani hosts banged gavel declared deal done, Chandni Raina, representative India, world' most populous country, tore  them, saying process  "stage managed."
" paltry sum," Ms. Raina said. " am sorry say  cannot accept. We seek much higher ambition developed countries." She called agreement "nothing  optical illusion."
Speakers  developing country after another, Bolivia Nigeria Fiji, echoed Ms. Raina' remarks assailed document furious statements.
"Let me crystal clear," said Juan Carlos Monterrey, Panama' special envoy climate. "This process chaotic, poorly managed, complete failure terms delivering ambition required."
financing negotiations complicated election Donald J. Trump less  week before summit' opening day. world' biggest economy, significant sway  global financial institutions, United States essential meeting climate finance pledges.
Mr. Trump widely expected renege any commitments negotiated Baku said  withdraw United States Paris Agreement, landmark 2015 climate accord aims curb global greenhouse gas emissions.

Another difficulty expectation Republican-led Congress would curtail funding Ukraine, placing greater burden country' European allies leaving less money available climate efforts.
Many negotiators diplomats said, however, Mr. Trump' election   created sense urgency around need speed transformation increasingly interconnected economies around world, many  still largely dependent fossil fuels like coal, oil gas.
" think  came here question, 'Does U.. no juice?'" said John Podesta, President Biden' climate envoy. said  team  "extremely active negotiations", "notwithstanding president-elect' rhetoric around calling climate change hoax," United States would continue reducing  emissions  Mr. Trump' term office.
agreement,   legally binding functions largely through diplomatic peer pressure, came after two weeks divisive debate   should pay how much.
Under U.N. rules written 1992, certain wealthy countries, mostly West, considered developed, while  nations, including China Saudi Arabia, considered developing. Countries developing group "invited" provide financial aid   expected do .
Today, however, many wealthy nations say distinction no longer makes sense China, Saudi Arabia others should compelled provide share climate financing. Western efforts move those countries  developed category met fierce resistance ultimately unsuccessful.
Developing countries  accused Western nations betraying  past commitments failing hit previous $100 billion target until years after deadline set under agreement. They  accused rich countries using domestic politics excuse contribute less.
negotiations,   scheduled end Friday  wrapped  just before sunrise Sunday, took place near end another  record-breaking heat. Global greenhouse gas emissions soared record 57 gigatons last ,   track decline much,,  decade, according U.N. report issued just before summit,  known COP29.

Over past five years, leaders European Union tried turn 27-country bloc  global climate front-runner.
They made big strides. They enshrined  law ambitious target cut planet-heating emissions  half 2030. They set 2035 deadline sales new gas-guzzling cars. They expanded price industries must pay emitting greenhouse gases.
But voters went polls recent days, Europe' green credentials faced very different test.
There widespread frustration  rising prices. Farmers' groups stormed European capitals protest proposals limit pollution agriculture. right wing ascendant. Greens,  2019 won  largest share seats European parliamentary elections, today polling poorly.
Europe loses  green groove, could far-reaching consequences  only European citizens businesses,   rest world. Europe among history' top polluters.
"There lot stake," Laurence Tubiana,  key architects Paris climate accord now head European Climate Foundation, wrote email. " gains last five years cannot taken granted."
Here five takeaways Europe' election.
climate crisis faced new crises.
2019 elections, European Greens scored 10 percent seats 705-member parliament, making them force contend leading conservative European People' Party.
time, zeitgeist green. Climate protesters  filled streets European capital cities, demanding action.
European Union soon approved European Green Deal, legally binding target reduce  emissions 55 percent 2030, compared 1990 levels.
Then, three big things happened. pandemic. Inflation. Russian invasion Ukraine. Almost overnight, war forced European countries ditch natural gas piped Russia, , until then, cheap source electricity.
bloc' economic powerhouse, Germany, felt impact acutely,  much  government' efforts accelerate adoption heat pumps became embroiled culture wars. Conservatives right-wing politicians, supported populist press, railed against what parties characterized, somewhat falsely, ban gas boilers. government  back off tweak  proposal.
But warming didn' stop being problem.
Europe warming faster  global average. hazards plain display: fires Greece, floods Germany, crippling heat waves Italy Spain.
Polls show robust support climate action,   concern costs signs what European Council Foreign Relations, research organization, calls "growing greenlash." While people "want action taken climate crisis,  do  want bear significant costs green transition themselves," organization wrote recent analysis.
vote  matter years.
immediate impact  bloc' emissions-reduction targets 2040.
current proposals European Commission, bloc' executive arm, calls cuts 90 percent 2040, compared 1990 levels. It' unclear what next commission might support, particularly because next round cuts  likely require altering things affect everyday lives, like home heating (hello, heat-pump controversy) transportation. Keep eye how 2035 ban sale new internal combustion cars  fare.
Perhaps toughest question  what do emissions agriculture.

Farmers' protests across Europe led current administration drop proposals limit pollution agriculture.
Looming  Europe' clean-energy-transition options choices being made United States China. Biden Administration showered green energy enterprises, battery factories carbon removal projects, tax incentives. China exporting low-cost solar panels, wind turbines, batteries electric vehicles worldwide.
Rystad Energy, private firm looks energy trends, pointed roughly $125 billion European Union invested clean energy technology would soon fall behind United States.
Politicians coloring Green Deal.
leading European People' Party claiming Green Deal  hallmark achievement, even whittles back unpopular provisions, like farming, eye ballot box. frames way sever Europe' dependence Russia. "We turned Putin' challenge  major new opportunity," European Commission president, Ursula Von der Leyen, said January.
Further right, European Conservatives Reformists party cast some Green Deal' policies - setting aside land restoration rather  agriculture, instance - culture wars issue says unfairly target farmers. promised examine what calls  election manifesto Green Deal' " problematic objectives."
Greens' message voters European businesses need clear signal  can compete green industries future. "These elections  determine future Europe' climate policy," Bas Eickhout, Green party leader said telephone. "  stop now, would bad news European industry."
Changes 'resilient' ( far).
Much renewable energy come online, putting European Union track draw 70 percent  electricity wind solar 2030, according E3G, research group. European law puts price climate pollution several industries. European carmakers, albeit belatedly, going electric.

"Right now, coal' future two-part story: What do  do currently operating coal plants, then, how do  make sure last coal plant  ever exist  that' already built," said Flora Champenois,  authors report. " weren' China boom, that' pretty much where we'd already."

Since April, heat waves, most likely fueled climate change, reached dangerous levels across India  Asian countries. This week, many Indian cities, including New Delhi, capital, recorded temperatures above 115 degrees Fahrenheit. Local governments sent heat alerts warning people avoid staying outside schools several states ordered close.
It' happening hundreds millions Indians head polls expected re-elect Prime Minister Narendra Modi.
grueling heat crisis,  several India  last few years, made me wonder nation' leaders  made climate change central  campaigns.
Not really, my colleague Suhasini Raj,  covers India, told me.
"  election manifestoes, India' top political parties, including governing Bharatiya Janata Party main opposition, Congress party, made multiple promises act climate damage reduce emissions," she said. "But climate pledges paper absent speeches rallies campaign trails."
Still, Raj pointed, Indians suffering effects climate change acutely  most. "Climate change pollution lead loss livelihoods, forced migrations kill  two million Indians annually," she said. " projected 148.3 million people India  living severe climate hot spots 2050."
Measuring impact heat can difficult: Estimates how many deaths can attributed heat India vary few hundred tens thousands . Official tolls often fail capture full lethality extreme temperatures.
Today, want explain how India adapting extreme heat, even climbing temperatures making  transition cleaner economy even difficult. country  short good ideas. But scaling them world' most populous nation still challenge.
rush adapt
India first country South Asia create heat action plan. started city Ahmedabad after extreme heat wave May 2010 resulted 43 percent increase mortality compared same period previous years, Somini Sengupta wrote.
plan involved establishing system warn residents dangerous temperatures expected, educating health care workers recognize heat-related illnesses opening cooling centers. Research estimates these programs save  1,000 lives .
Since 2010, India' national government worked  20 states  130 cities establish  own heat action plans. results mixed. Many plans aren' public, fail reach vulnerable populations lack resources implement solutions.
But local governments trying variety strategies. asked Vishwas Chitale, Council Energy, Environment Water, think tank based Delhi, list some most successful.
mentioned Telangana' statewide policy help paint tile roofs white colors can reflect sunlight cool buildings, new heatstroke wards big hospitals workshop run India' National Disaster Management Authority help local officials prepare extreme heat.
lot work supported Natural Resources Defense Council, advocacy group. Vijay Limaye, climate health scientist  follows India' heat policies group, told me country' experience showed  "some tested solutions." "But  need deployed rapidly scale," said.
India' difficult energy transition
India  fastest-growing big economy world  dependence coal produce electricity major source concern struggle curb climate change.
India' bind: needs replace coal even  energy needs growing quickly, along  economy. extreme temperatures create even bigger burden, country often forced burn coal keep cooling devices running across country.
This problem may only get difficult future. Scientists say global temperatures  continue rise, though only 9 percent Indians air-conditioning units now, number likely increase ninefold 2050.
good news, recent years, renewable energy capacity growing fast, said Kaushik Deb,  runs India program Columbia University' Center Global Energy Policy.
first quarter  , example, 71.5 percent new power capacity added India came renewable energy, according analysis Institute Energy Economics Financial Analysis. report  noted coal accounted less  half India' total capacity first time since 1960s.
Making sure India' energy needs don' grow  necessary  part answer. means investing efficient buildings appliances, public transportation electric vehicles, some  already part India' development plan.
Until India can come  "sensible reasonable adaptation policy heat wave-kind events," Deb said, " demand electricity  continue rise."

decades, oil gas, construction real estate industries complained permitting requirements hindered business. Communities affected major projects environmental groups often able use NEPA delay prevent projects moving forward.

Nearly day,  confounding news make us question whether world pushing hard enough transition away fossil fuels.
Yesterday, Chevron announced $53 billion acquisition midsize rival, Big Oil consolidates doubles down bet fossil fuel demand  continue increase.
But you zoom just bit, picture may bit clearer. This morning, International Energy Agency predicted global demand oil, natural gas coal  peak seven years, my colleague Brad Plumer reported. Solar, wind, hydropower, electric vehicles heat pumps projected surge.
separate study came last week suggests world may reached " global irreversible solar tipping point," ever-cheaper solar dominating electricity markets purely because market forces, without any additional climate policies.
past few months, we've telling you U.. energy transition that' arriving faster  you think. But move toward solar global: study' authors expect solar cheapest source electricity almost countries 2027..E..' projections come pass, oil gas demand would most likely plateau slightly above today' levels next three decades, expanding developing countries shrinking advanced economies. Demand coal, dirtiest fossil fuels, would start declining, though might fluctuate  , say, coal plants needed run often during heat waves droughts.
" transition clean energy happening worldwide it' unstoppable," Fatih Birol, executive director International Energy Agency, told Brad.
cleanest fuel cheapest
Cost bedrock solar revolution. Between 2010 2020, study found, cost solar cells fell 15 percent each . Those staggering numbers.
" pace decline price initially surprised many people," my colleague Ivan Penn,  covers energy sector, told me.
Starting 2000s, China pumped billions dollars  solar technology tried reduce  dependence foreign energy. boom production, ruthless competition scientific innovation followed, caused price panels plummet, even many companies driven business.
 around time California started heavily subsidize rooftop solar. quarter state' electricity now comes solar.
According recent report Lazard, financial advisory management firm, unsubsidized solar photovoltaic onshore wind power now 48 percent cheaper  next cheapest energy source, natural gas.

Poland Spring' hidden attack Maine water law
Maine lawmakers tried tighten regulations large-scale access water, company behind Poland Spring  big bottled water brands set rewrite rules.
Bottlers faced increasing scrutiny millions throwaway plastic bottles  produce, marketing message  products safer healthier  tap water, business model   buy freshwater, often low cost, only sell back public much higher prices.
company called BlueTriton owns many nation' biggest brands, including Poland Spring,  named after natural spring Maine ran dry decades ago.

Electric heat pumps, most affordable energy efficient way heat cool homes, continue outsell gas furnaces nationwide. They can  reduce outdoor pollution, result, save lives, according report issued Tuesday.
study, Rewiring America, nonprofit group promotes electrification, calculated American household got rid furnaces, hot water heaters clothes dryers powered oil gas replaced them heat pumps electric appliances, annual greenhouse gas emissions could drop 400 million metric tons. Fine airborne particulate matter  air pollutants could decrease 300,000 tons, equivalent taking 40 million cars off road.
Roughly two-thirds country' households burn fossil fuels such natural gas, propane fuel oil heat, hot water drying clothes, releasing nitrogen oxides  pollutants  air.
While transition electric appliances could shave $60 billion off people' annual energy bills, could  deliver important health rewards, researchers found. could prevent 3,400 fewer premature deaths per , 1,300 fewer hospital visits 220,000 fewer asthma attacks,  amounted $40 billion benefits, according study.
"Just swapping appliances, it' eye-opening terms significant impacts," said Wael Kanj, senior research associate Rewiring America lead author report.
amount pollution reduction would depend whether electricity needed generated low-carbon sources like wind solar power came gas coal-fired power plants.
" report rightly highlights need produce clean energy," Rob Jackson, climate scientist Stanford University  researched health impacts gas stoves, wrote email. "Electrifying our homes  fewer benefits West Virginia, where around 90 percent electricity comes dirty coal,   states cleaner electricity."
Mr. Kanj said grid decarbonized faster  forecast, pollution reductions health benefits would increase.
Yifang Zhu, professor U.C.L.. Fielding School Public Health expert air pollution, said ways household appliances affected outdoor air pollution  largely gone understudied new research helped fill gap. " sector needs looked," she said. "People can realize  benefits  just improved indoor air quality electrifying households."
effect gas stoves indoor air came  sharp focus  past  after study linked  use  19,000 deaths United States 50,000 cases childhood asthma.
American households moving heat pumps,  outsold gas furnaces recent years pace do  again 2024.
report Rewiring America, titled "Breathe Easy," drew data, analyses reports series government agencies, including National Renewable Energy Laboratory, Environmental Protection Agency, U.. Census Bureau Energy Information Administration.
American Gas Association, industry group, contested findings. spokeswoman, Emily Carlin, pointed association' own research,  found cost less use natural gas new home compared going -electric. group' research found high-efficiency natural gas appliances could yield fewer greenhouse gas emissions compared -electric household said reductions greenhouse gas emissions similar heat pump colder climate.
association  highlighted study funded World Health Organization found no significant increased asthma risk home gas use compared electricity, lower risk bronchitis, though study did find possible increased risk pneumonia obstructive pulmonary disease.
Amy Andryszak, president chief executive Interstate Natural Gas Association America, called study "inherently flawed" said  calculations did  include increased costs electric grid would come getting rid natural gas. "This  serious study,  rather attempt secure headlines same false misleading talking points activists using years," she said.
Under Inflation Reduction Act, federal government allocated nearly $9 billion home energy rebates electric appliances energy efficient home retrofits. 2023,  250,000 families claimed federal tax credits heat pumps  100,000 families claimed credits heat pump water heaters. President-elect Donald J. Trump said wants repeal law. It' unclear what could mean heat pump adoption, many rebate funds already allocated states.

unlikely scene: Sultan Al Jaber, chairman  world' biggest oil companies, stood before negotiators nearly 200 nations Wednesday heralded major new climate agreement pledges transition global economy away fossil fuels.
language reminiscent award acceptance speech, Mr. Al Jaber congratulated gathered diplomats, himself, proving doubters wrong. After three decades United Nations climate deals neglected even mention fossil fuels like oil gas, presiding  summit explicitly targeted main cause global warming.
"Many said  could  done," Mr. Al Jaber said.
Mr. Al Jaber, 50, contentious choice lead annual climate negotiations,  normally led politician diplomat host country. United Arab Emirates,  year' host, selected him last January. While does hold several government positions, Mr. Al Jaber largely dedicated running Abu Dhabi National Oil Company.

Building New Industries
Loan Programs Office created 2005 help emerging energy technologies,  often trouble getting conventional financing, become commercially viable. gained notoriety after lent $535 million 2009 Solyndra, solar firm went bankrupt two years later. But  gave crucial $465 million loan 2010 Tesla,  later grew  electric vehicle powerhouse.
Mostly dormant during first Trump administration, office revived under President Biden. initially  $40 billion loan authority,  ballooned  $400 billion 2022 Congress passed Inflation Reduction Act.

program access thousands experts Energy Department  can scrutinize novel technologies commercial banks find too bewildering.

office  backed novel technologies could ultimately prove valuable, such plant South Dakota could turn cornstarch  jet fuel, company uses lasers spot methane leaks oil gas wells.

increase  uniform across globe. Emissions  most likely decline   United States Europe, fossil fuel use China slowed. Yet offset surge carbon dioxide India rest world.
findings made public early Wednesday United Nations climate change summit Baku, Azerbaijan, where diplomats world leaders gathered discuss how raise trillions dollars cope rising global temperatures. last year' climate talks Dubai, United Arab Emirates, representatives nearly nation approved pact called "transitioning away fossil fuels" accelerating climate action  decade. relatively small number countries account majority world' emissions, China responsible 32 percent, United States 13 percent, India 8 percent European Union 6 percent.
Europe' emissions expected decline roughly 3.8 percent  , report said. That' partly because wind solar power rapidly displaced coal electricity sector. United States, carbon dioxide emissions expected drop modestly  , around 0.6 percent. America' emissions coal now  lowest levels  120 years utilities continue retire  older coal-burning power plants.
same time, however, U.. electricity demand soared record highs result scorching summer heat waves rapid build- data centers. led record demand natural gas,  emits half much carbon dioxide coal burned energy.
China, world' largest emitter, saw small increase emissions   around 0.2 percent. But experts say too soon say whether Chinese emissions might verge peaking.
recent years, China built solar arrays, wind farms electric vehicles  any  country. same time, China' rate economic growth slowed, sectors like construction heavy industry cooling off after decades rapid expansion.

rest world, emissions increased roughly 1.1 percent  . International air travel shipping  increased rapidly, though both still below levels 2019, before coronavirus pandemic.

latest piece our series energy transition takes close look growing interest small nuclear plants.
Around country, nearly dozen companies developing reactors significantly smaller  conventional nuclear power plants. hope   easier build, permits can issued quickly, they'll substantially cheaper  earlier nuclear plants, my colleagues Brad Plumer Ivan Penn report.
technology could provide steady electricity grid supplement intermittent wind solar energy. But industry faces substantial hurdles.
Beyond persistent concerns nuclear waste, industry struggling find profitable path forward. Last week, first major effort build small reactors United States abruptly canceled amid soaring costs.
