During scorching summer 2023, Texas energy grid wobbled surging demand electricity threatened exceed supply. Several times, officials called residents conserve energy avoid grid failure.
This  turned much better - thanks large part renewable energy.
electrical grid Texas breezed through summer , despite milder temperatures, state again reached record levels energy demand. did  largely thanks substantial expansion new solar farms.
grid held strong even during critical early evening hours - sun goes down nighttime winds yet pick  - help even newer source energy Texas around country: batteries.
federal government expects amount battery storage capacity across country, almost nonexistent five years ago, nearly double end . Texas,  already surpassed California amount power coming large-scale solar farms, expected gain  West Coast rival battery storage.
swift growth battery storage source power electric grid, along continued expansion large-scale solar farms, could  come better time. Texas, like many  states, facing surge  power needs data centers, new manufacturing plants, cryptocurrency mines, growing residential demand increasingly intense summer heat. Officials estimate Texas, already nation' largest electricity consumer, could roughly double  demand just few years.
" state going go through . Texas just happens farthest along because  growing our energy usage first," said Michael Lee, chief executive Octopus Energy U.., subsidiary British electricity provider. "We're seeing   state,  world."
Renewable energy sources taken off Texas, nation' premier oil--gas state, large part because relatively easy process connecting state' grid  mostly deregulated market energy, company leaders energy experts said.
But some those same advantages  come under scrutiny since grid' catastrophic failure during prolonged winter freeze killed hundreds state 2021.
Gov. Greg Abbott took steps try prevent similar failure happening again. Since then, leaders Texas Capitol, along energy companies types, debating how ensure  always enough energy supply, while  preserving state' relatively free market.
Since winter storm, Mr. Abbott, state' lieutenant governor, Dan Patrick, pushed construction new gas-fired power plants, arguing predictable, "dispatchable" energy reliable emergency  wind solar.
energy market Texas, mostly deregulated 2000s, generators paid, some exceptions, only energy  actually provide grid, rather  having capacity do  future. contrast  states, main electricity grid Texas contained entirely within state.
somewhat unlikely turn, renewable energy companies arguing keeping market open less regulated, while Republican leaders pushed increasing government intervention, including incentives build additional gas-fired power plants.
" abject irony once-freewheeling power market now precipice becoming fully government-controlled," said Aaron Zubaty, chief executive Eolian,  invests energy projects. "New technologies provided market solutions scaled rapidly," said. result, added, legacy gas-fired power generators  political backers trying increase barriers newcomers "push market go - natural gas."
pointed swift growth batteries, even without any state mandates, example why interventions  necessary bring innovation.

CLIMATE FIX
big geothermal project moves forward
problem: Decarbonizing grid means moving away fossil fuels,  currently make  60 percent utility-scale electricity generation United States.
fix: Enhanced geothermal systems (E.G..) extension traditional geothermal energy use hydraulic fracturing, fracking, make cracks underground rock access Earth' heat energy source. Using fracking techniques adapted oil gas industry allows E.G.. tap geothermal energy wider range locations.
"They avoid lot drilling problems,  make  own fractures way  want them, gives them much predictable outcome," said Roland Horne, earth sciences professor Stanford University.
Unlike wind solar energy, geothermal systems can provide power 24 hours day.
Last month, Bureau Land Management approved plan advanced geothermal project Beaver County, Utah, Fervo Energy,  -known startups deploying  technology. (We last wrote Fervo 2023.)
company' approach unique: After drilling thousands feet deep underground, then drills horizontally access additional rock.
Fervo' Utah plan aims supply much two gigawatts' worth power starting 2026,  enough power  two million homes fully developed. company recently started constructing power plant infrastructure  need supply energy customers, company' CEO, Tim Latimer, said.
obstacles: One longtime concern geothermal energy  potential cause earthquakes, what scientists refer "induced seismicity." Some those earthquakes  minor  aren' felt humans aboveground,  certain cases geothermal projects caused significant damage. Stanford' Horne says earthquakes remains " big kind bugaboo E.G.."
Latimer said Fervo monitoring seismic activity deep underground around  operations. activity surpasses certain thresholds, company can halt operations, said.
Another big obstacle these projects face access electrical grid. Utah, Fervo chose location where  access existing transmission lines, Latimer said.
"Probably thing that' going limit our ambitious growth plans most going access transmission," said.
Geothermal can  costly. Latimer said Fervo lowered both drilling costs time takes drill geothermal project 70 percent.
Environmentalists, federal government others  expressed concerns geothermal projects negatively affecting air quality, water resources hot springs.
What' next: Fervo planning second phase  Utah project running 2028. Geothermal energy last  only accounted 0.4 percent country' electricity,  Latimer said hoped industry would make  close 1 percent country' electricity generation 2030. - Allison Prang
2024 temperatures track record high, researchers find
This   almost certainly hottest  record, beating high set 2023, researchers announced Wednesday.
assessment, Copernicus Climate Change Service, European Union agency monitors global warming,  forecast 2024 would first calendar   global temperatures consistently rose 1.5 degrees Celsius above preindustrial levels.
That' temperature threshold countries agreed, Paris Agreement, planet should avoid crossing. Beyond amount warming, scientists say, Earth  face irreversible damage. - Austyn Gaffney

Uruguay' energy revolution
Uruguay, nation 3.4 million people wedged between Argentina Brazil, generates nearly  electricity renewable sources. 2008, government set goal transforming electric grid,   come depend imported oil.
country  lot hydropower,  years drought 1990s 2000s slashed dams' output. Uruguay forced import oil instead, volatile prices, faced shortages blackouts. Officials noted increasing cost competitiveness renewables, especially wind, set build local wind industry nearly scratch.
Between 2013 2018, wind generation grew sharply almost nothing quarter Uruguay' electricity mix. end 2022, most recent  data available, Uruguay generated  90 percent  power renewables, wind solar growing even hydropower declined.
This small nation represents  especially fast example massive growth renewable energy globally.
Electricity heat together biggest source humans' greenhouse gas emissions. But "many, many countries now," renewables growing faster  electricity demand displacing fossil fuels power sector, said Bill Hare, C.E.O. senior scientist Climate Analytics, international climate science policy organization. " got most potential next five years get us onto  half degree pathway anything close."
Transportation second biggest source greenhouse gas emissions. Electric car sales grown exponentially  past decade, China far largest market these vehicles. 7.3 million battery electric vehicles sold around world 2022, according International Energy Agency.  half these cars, 4.4 million, sold China.
Historically, megacities like Shanghai driven  trend. But recent years China' smaller cities started taking  larger share market. 2022, two cities where electric vehicles made  largest percentage total new car registrations Sanya, city beach resorts Hainan Island, Liuzhou, industrial hub southern China. Battery electric vehicles accounted 40 percent new vehicles registrations both cities, far above national average 19 percent, according recent report International Council Clean Transportation.
Electric vehicles' success China hinged partially policy, partially sheer convenience affordability. most popular electric car China currently Hongguang Mini, tiny two-door model costs $5,000. It' manufactured three-way international joint venture SAIC-GM-Wuling, factories Liuzhou.
Paris, city bikes
Some cities trying  just electrify cars,  replace many them possible cleaner forms transportation, like bicycles. 2021, officials Paris announced plan make  city "100 percent cycle-friendly" next five years.
Paris already yearslong journey do away cars city center, least reduce  numbers. Between 2001 2018, number car trips taken Paris fell 60 percent. Over same period, public transit trips increased 40 percent bicycle trips 20 percent.
Cycling boomed even recent years, spurred part new bike lanes set  during coronavirus pandemic, nicknamed "coronapistes," "corona lanes." percentage trips taken bicycle within Paris  doubled between 2020 2024, 5 11 percent, according Paris Region Institute, urban planning agency works cities around Europe.
Paris currently  1,000 kilometers bike lanes,  get 180 under current plan, along tens thousands bicycle parking spots new traffic light patterns prioritize cyclists public transit.

One  after world leaders made landmark promise move away fossil fuels, countries essentially made no progress cutting emissions tackling global warming, according United Nations report issued Thursday.
Global greenhouse gas emissions soared record 57 gigatons last   track decline much,,  decade, report found. Collectively, nations  slow curtail  use oil, gas coal now looks unlikely countries  able limit global warming levels  agreed under 2015 Paris climate agreement.
"Another  passed without action means we're worse off," said Anne Olhoff, climate policy expert based Denmark co-author assessment, known Emissions Gap Report.
report comes month before diplomats around world scheduled meet Baku, Azerbaijan, annual United Nations climate talks, where countries  discuss how  might step  efforts address global warming.
Lately, those efforts faced huge obstacles.
Even though renewable energy sources like wind solar growing rapidly around world, demand electricity rising even faster,  means countries still burning fossil fuels each . Geopolitical conflicts, U..-China rivalry war places like Ukraine Gaza, made international cooperation climate change harder. rich countries failed keep  financial promises help poor countries shift away oil, gas coal.
last year' climate talks Dubai, United Arab Emirates, representatives nearly nation approved pact called "transitioning away fossil fuels" accelerating climate action  decade. But agreement vague how do   countries should do what,  far  little follow-through.
new U.N. report finds least 151 countries formally pledged under Paris climate agreement curb  greenhouse gas emissions 2030. country followed through  stated plans,  far assured, then global emissions could 3 percent 11 percent lower end decade   today.
But would still put Earth track heat  average roughly 2.6 2.8 degrees Celsius (4.7 5 degrees Fahrenheit)  preindustrial levels century' end, report found. planet already warmed roughly 1.3 degrees Celsius (2.3 degrees Fahrenheit). may  sound like much,  fraction degree warming brings greater risks deadly heat waves, wildfires, drought, storms species extinction, scientists said.
Under Paris Agreement, world leaders vowed hold global warming " below" 2 degrees Celsius, preferably closer 1.5 degrees Celsius, limit risks climate catastrophes.
Current policies don' come close meeting those goals, report found. stay below 2 degrees Celsius, global emissions would need fall roughly 28 percent between now 2030. stay 1.5 degrees Celsius, global emissions would need fall 43 percent, requiring blindingly fast transformation global energy system.
Over past , only island nation Madagascar submitted new, stronger pledge curb emissions 2030, despite exhortations United Nations officials countries bolster  plans. each  goes without additional action, cuts needed hold warming those low levels become extreme.
"Theoretically, it' still possible stay below 1.5 degrees,  it'  really feasible anymore," said Christoph Bertram, associate research professor University Maryland' Center Global Sustainability.
Inger Andersen, executive director United Nations Environment Program, said still important nations accelerate  efforts cut emissions keep warming low possible.
"Even world overshoots 1.5 degrees Celsius, chances  happening increasing day,  must keep striving" bring emissions down zero soon possible, Ms. Andersen said. " fraction degree avoided counts terms lives saved, economies protected, damages avoided, biodiversity conserved."
coming , countries expected submit new formal targets cutting emissions 2035. remains seen how ambitious those goals  whether countries  take concrete steps follow through.
One big topics climate talks Baku  money. years, developing countries like India Indonesia said  would willing accelerate efforts cut emissions  received financial assistance wealthier countries do . According U.N. report, cutting global emissions zero could require extra $900 billion $1.2 trillion per  global investment.
amount, report said, "substantial"   manageable broader context global economy financial markets worth close $110 trillion.

new rule Federal Energy Regulatory Commission,  oversees interstate electricity transmission, most significant attempt years upgrade expand country' creaking electricity network. Experts warned  aren' nearly enough high-voltage power lines being built today, putting country greater risk blackouts extreme weather while making harder shift renewable sources energy cope rising electricity demand.
big reason slow pace grid expansion operators rarely plan long term, commission said.
nation' three main electric grids overseen patchwork utilities regional grid operators mainly focus ensuring reliability electricity homes businesses. comes building new transmission lines, grid operators tend reactive, responding after wind-farm developer asks connect existing network once reliability problem spotted.
new federal rule,  two years making, requires grid operators around country identify needs 20 years  future, taking  account factors like changes energy mix, growing number states require wind solar power risks extreme weather.

What FERC, Exactly?
Federal Energy Regulatory Commission may most important climate agency you've never heard.
commission approved rule 2-1 vote, two Democratic commissioners favor lone Republican, Mark Christie, opposed. Mr. Christie said rule would allow states want renewable energy unfairly pass costs necessary grid upgrades  neighbors.

#225
Countries made scant progress curbing  greenhouse gas emissions  past , keeping planet track dangerous levels warming  century, according new report published Thursday.
report Climate Action Tracker, research group, estimates climate energy policies currently pursued governments around world would cause global temperatures rise roughly 2.7 degrees Celsius, 4.9 degrees Fahrenheit, above preindustrial levels 2100.
estimate future warming barely budged three years now, group said.
"We clearly failing bend curve," said Sofia Gonzales-Zuñiga, climate policy specialist Climate Analytics, science policy organization, lead author report. " world edges closer these dangerous climate thresholds, need immediate, stronger action reverse  trend becomes ever urgent."
study issued during United Nations climate summit Baku, Azerbaijan, where diplomats world leaders gathered discuss how raise trillions dollars cope rising global temperatures.
Under 2015 Paris Agreement, world leaders  pledged hold total global warming " below" 2 degrees Celsius, preferably closer 1.5 degrees Celsius, limit risks climate catastrophes. Scientists said fraction degree warming brings greater risks deadly heat waves, wildfires, drought, storms species extinction.
stringent target looks increasingly reach.
, Climate Action Tracker scrutinizes climate policies countries enacted worldwide, such regulations curb pollution power plants improve efficiency cars. They then estimate effect these policies future greenhouse gas emissions calculate how much temperature increase world can expect.
Over past three years, United States enacted Inflation Reduction Act,  expected pour hundreds billions dollars  low-carbon technologies like wind, solar nuclear energy, carbon capture technology. China selling record numbers electric vehicles. European Union ramped   targets renewable energy heat pumps.
Yet world still heading significant warming because global energy demand growing faster  clean energy expanding,  means fossil fuel use rising fill gap.
"Rising emissions while renewables boom  paradox," said Niklas Höhne, scientist NewClimate Institute,  partners Climate Action Tracker. " recent years fossil fuels won race against renewables, leading increasing emissions."
part global climate talks, many countries formally pledged zero  emissions around midcentury. governments followed through, warming might limited roughly 2.1 degrees Celsius, report said. But many countries  backed  those lofty promises concrete action.
Climate Action Tracker  tried estimate potential effects Donald J. Trump' return White House. promised slash support clean energy electric vehicles while dismantling environmental regulations.
authors calculated complete rollback U.. climate policies might add much few tenths degree warming 2100. effect would relatively limited, part because United States accounts just 13 percent global emissions today.
 hand, Trump administration' actions led  countries weaken abandon  climate policies, effect might far greater.
study  calculated what countries would need do hold total warming closer long-term average 1.5 degrees Celsius, leaders pledged. United States would need slash  emissions roughly 80 percent below 2005 levels 2035. China would need cut  emissions two-thirds  same time frame. India, Europe, Brazil, Japan Australia would make deeper cuts   currently planning.
This , Earth  already 1.5 degrees Celsius hotter  preindustrial levels, least temporarily. climate talks Baku, many world leaders reluctant acknowledge world overwhelmingly likely exceed threshold years ahead.
But some scientists speaking.
" always possible find arguments make 1.5°C forever possible,   increasingly diverge reality," wrote Glen Peters, senior researcher CICERO Center International Climate Research Oslo, paper published  week. " time admit world  cross 1.5°C."
same time, Dr. Peters wrote, crossing threshold "does  mean world failed." Scientists said climate change risks increase fraction degree,   always worthwhile cut emissions quickly feasible prevent further warming. But, added, focusing unrealistic temperature limit " no longer useful."
"Crossing 1.5°C  time give ," wrote, " time acknowledge our failures find new hope moving forward."

#255
California air quality regulators tightened carbon standards attempt further cut emissions fuels state' transportation system.
Several members California Air Resources Board, powerful body can influence global policies, called rule changes critical step climate amid expected rollback federal environmental efforts under President-elect Donald J. Trump.
But complex regulations,  approved end marathon meeting Friday, spurred political fights   between various groups. State lawmakers both parties criticized changes  potential hike California' gas prices,  already among highest nation.
new restrictions  triggered dispute among environmental groups, researchers industry officials  whether California program disproportionately benefits biofuels sector.
Here' what know changes.
How California program changing?
California' Low Carbon Fuel Standard statewide, market-based program requires significant cuts carbon emissions produced transportation fuels. This includes emissions associated  production, conveyance use consumers. transportation sector whole accounts half state' greenhouse gas emissions.
comply, fuel producers must either reduce emissions  own operations buy credits companies sell fuels considered low carbon. Such fuels include electricity electric vehicles renewable diesel, biofuel chemically equivalent petroleum diesel  derived renewable biological sources such plant oils animal fats.
Fuel producers previously required, 2030, cut carbon emissions   fuel' life cycle 20 percent below 2010 levels. 12-2 vote end 12-hour meeting, regulators tightened these standards, raising 2030 target 30 percent adding 2045 target 90 percent reduction.
"It' improving what incredibly successful program," said Liane Randolph, board chairwoman.
updates  included additional funding zero-emission vehicle charging new sustainability requirements renewable fuels.
Will these changes increase gas prices California?
key point tension concern changes could significantly raise gas prices. Oil companies already pass 8 10 cents per gallon onto consumers result Low Carbon Fuel Standard program, according agency.
Last September, agency estimated earlier version proposal could hiked gas prices 47 cents per gallon starting next  could potentially added $1.80 per gallon 2040. However, officials said estimates did  take  account many important factors affect gas prices  did  reflect changes ultimately passed.
"We lot data analysis  published makes very clear difficult,  impossible, calculate exactly how program like  affects retail gas prices," Ms. Randolph said.
Ms. Randolph added program would create competition transportation sector,  would give consumers choice fuels  want spend money. would  keep transportation costs lower overall consumers, she said.
Danny Cullenward, climate economist Kleinman Center Energy Policy University Pennsylvania, released analysis amendments found  could increase gas prices much $0.85 per gallon 2030 nearly $1.50 per gallon 2035. Mr. Cullenward said wrote report independent academic project;  member state advisory committee prohibits him having financial conflicts interest companies participate California' carbon market.
"We talking program politically radioactive," said.
Mr. Cullenward added agency' decision  release estimate gas price impacts before final vote last week could reduce trust state regulators hinder California' ability approve further climate-related changes future.
Members both sides aisle California' state assembly warned higher gas prices could hurt  constituents. Nearly 13,000 residents signed petition led Republicans State Senate urged regulators postpone vote amendments.
State Sen. Melissa Hurtado, moderate Democrat, said many her constituents Central Valley lower-income relied heavily  vehicles transportation.
" time people already struggling make ends meet,  slap face," Ms. Hurtado said.
Western States Petroleum Association  warned changes could increase costs. " end day, consumers  ones  impacted most," said Jodie Muller, group' chief operating officer.
How   affect California' climate goals?
California Air Resources Board estimates program eliminated 320 million metric tons carbon dioxide emissions gasoline diesel since began 2011, amount equivalent roughly 85 percent state' annual greenhouse gas emissions.
agency projecting  tighter restrictions  reduce greenhouse gas emissions 558 million metric tons between 2025 2045 - equivalent  half times state' annual emissions.
Ethan Elkind, director climate program Center Law, Energy Environment University California, Berkeley, said changes would "absolutely" make significant dent reducing transportation emissions nudging people away gasoline providing incentives move toward -electric alternatives.
California  tried reduce fossil-fuel use through consumer product restrictions. state   banned sale new gas-powered lawn mowers blowers. state  required new cars sold produce zero emissions starting 2035, policy championed Gov. Gavin Newsom.
Why some environmentalists opposed changes?
Some researchers environmental groups raised concerns program' focus biofuels.
Some climate scientists said program does  adequately take  account emissions associated production biofuels, such underestimating those deforestation  parts world supports agriculture used make them.
Adrian Martinez, deputy managing attorney Earthjustice, environmental law organization, said program  "biofuel boondoggle"  majority program' financial incentives  gone toward biofuels instead electrification.
"We're  asking perfection," said. "There plenty opportunity do,  didn' take."
Ms. Randolph said board  directed staff 2025 further review climate impact biofuels better understand  any changes would necessary.
What comes next?
board' approval, changes  now reviewed state' Office Administrative Law. board expects changes take effect spring 2025, according Ms. Randolph.
Since Low Carbon Fuel Standard California state program, federal government no authority  changes, Ms. Randolph said.

leader State Senate, Mike McGuire, seemed suggest statement Legislature could still intervene.

Biden administration plans spend  $6 billion new technologies cut carbon dioxide emissions heavy industries like steel, cement, chemicals aluminum,  enormous contributors global warming    far incredibly difficult clean .
Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm said Monday her agency would partially fund 33 different projects 20 states test methods curbing emissions wide variety factories industrial plants, calling " single largest industrial decarbonization investment American history."
Constellium, aluminum producer, would receive  $75 million build first---kind aluminum casting plant Ravenswood, W.Va., can run cleaner burning hydrogen fuels rather  natural gas.
Kraft Heinz, food manufacturer, would get  $170.9 million install electric boilers heat pumps 10 facilities across country, where  would used generate large amounts heat needed things like drying macaroni without directly burning fossil fuels.
Cleveland-Cliffs, steel manufacturer, would get  $500 million help retire large coal-consuming blast furnace Middletown, Ohio, replace two furnaces use electricity turn scrap  steel. company would  test ways produce steel using hydrogen.
While projects themselves would put relatively small dent U.. emissions, Ms. Granholm said goal demonstrate novel technologies can scale  rapidly "set new gold standard clean manufacturing United States around world."
Heavy industry  nation' largest sources planet-warming pollution, accounting roughly -quarter emissions. Many factories burn coal natural gas produce heat needed create steam, temper glass turn iron  steel. Cement makers emit carbon dioxide part process transforming limestone  cement. Chemical producers use oil gas raw materials  products.
theory,  technologies can cut emissions. Industrial heat pumps thermal batteries could help factories generate heat renewable electricity. Cement makers could capture bury  carbon dioxide. Steel makers could use clean hydrogen instead coal. But many those solutions expensive  infancy.
"It' different electricity sector, where widely available alternatives fossil fuels like wind, solar batteries come down dramatically cost," Morgan Bazilian, professor public policy Colorado School Mines, said recent interview. " industry,  haven' yet seen clear winners emerge price needed."
Policymakers  hesitant crack down industrial emissions fear factories jobs could move abroad places looser environmental rules.

nears  scheduled completion  next few years, Bankside Yards project shops, offices 600 apartments  open  new public spaces make easier walk between attractions such Tate Modern Royal Festival Hall.

But 5.5-acre development, expected cost 2.5 billion British pounds ( $3.3 billion), greater significance  speeding  scenic pathway.
Hidden inside eight-building complex pioneering energy system poses important questions pace decarbonization United Kingdom elsewhere.
Bankside Yards using "fifth-generation" combined heating cooling network can balance energy within each building then between buildings collecting unwanted heat, say refrigerator restaurant piece office equipment needs cooled, carrying heat somewhere needs hot water domestic heating.
Electric-powered heat pumps building rooftops each apartment commercial space then adjust temperature water withdrawing injecting heat  pipes provide heating, cooling hot water needed each place.

first building complex, 19-story office tower called Arbor, completed 2023 already  75 percent rented under offer tenants, including environmental consultancy Carbon Trust.
fifth-generation network latest version district heating, approach commercially pioneered United States 1870s,  buildings heated through shared network pipes rather  having  own boilers stoves.
Early versions used central furnace distribute steam  390 degrees Fahrenheit, later moving extremely hot water before successive generations district heating lowered temperatures reduce amount heat lost pipes.
"Those high temperatures why corridors lobbies lot existing building always overheating - typical lose 20 percent 40 percent energy you're producing," said Giovanni Festa, project director Sweco, Swedish-based engineering consultancy,  oversaw design energy system Bankside Yards.
fifth-generation network  known ambient loop because water pipes  far above normal outside temperature - Bankside Yards water below 77 degrees Fahrenheit. complex' managers negotiated supply deals renewable electricity providers apartment heat pumps,  size small refrigerators hidden soundproofed cupboards.
Mr. Festa said energy requirements Arbor building  slashed high-quality window glazing design  used 30 percent less energy  current best practice standards.
After Bankside Yards reaches  scheduled completion 2029, facility managers  review performance data improve system' efficiency, weather forecasts  used fine-tune performance further.
" think we're least two three years ahead our competitors because  buildings being completed now still using gas-fired systems," Mr. Festa said.

report district heating systems Cardiff University' School Engineering identified 74 fifth-generation networks Germany, 16 Switzerland smaller numbers elsewhere, including three China.

Ali Hajimiri thinks there' better way power planet -  that'  getting attention deserves. Caltech professor electrical engineering envisages thousands solar panels floating space, unobstructed clouds unhindered day-night cycles, wirelessly transmitting massive amounts energy receivers Earth.
This , vision moved closer reality Mr. Hajimiri, together team Caltech researchers, proved wireless power transfer space possible: Solar panels   attached Caltech prototype space successfully converted electricity  microwaves beamed those microwaves receivers foot away, lighting  two LEDs.
prototype  beamed tiny  detectable amount energy receiver top  lab' building Pasadena, Calif. demonstration marks first step wireless transfer usable power space Earth - power source Mr. Hajimiri believes  safer  direct sun rays. " beam intensity kept less  solar intensity earth," said.
Finding alternative energy sources  topics  discussed leaders business, science public policy during New York Times Climate Forward event Thursday. Caltech demonstration significant moment quest realize space-based solar power - clean energy technology long overshadowed  long-shot clean energy ideas, such nuclear fusion low-cost clean hydrogen.
space-based solar can made work commercial scale, said Nikolai Joseph, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center senior technology analyst, such stations could contribute much 10 percent global power 2050.
idea space-based solar energy around since least 1941, science-fiction writer Isaac Asimov set   short stories, "Reason," solar station beamed energy microwaves Earth  planets.
1970s, fivefold increase oil prices sparked interest alternative energy, NASA Department Energy conducted first significant study topic. 1995, under direction physicist John C. Mankins, NASA took another look concluded investments space-launch technology needed lower cost before space-based solar power could realized.
"There never any doubt being technically feasible," said Mr. Mankins, now president Artemis Innovation Management Solutions, technology consulting group. " cost too prohibitive."
Today, however, calculus may changing.
advent Elon Musk' SpaceX brought steep decline cost rocket launches. 1970 2000, average low-earth-orbit rocket launch cost around $18,500 kilogram, 2.2 pounds, weight; today, cost plummeted low $1,500 kilogram. reduction helped drastically reduce estimates building power stations beyond Earth' atmosphere.
1980 review NASA concluded first gigawatt space-based solar power (enough energy power 100 million LED bulbs) would cost  $20 billion ($100 billion today). 1997, NASA estimated number  dropped $7 billion ($15 billion today); now, estimated closer $5 billion, according study conducted European Space Agency 2022.
" used critic space-based solar power," said Ramez Naam, climate clean energy investor. Mr. Naam now actively seeking space-based solar companies invest. " dramatically changing cost space launches changed everything," said.
Space-based solar power requires wirelessly transmitting electrical energy across space using microwave laser power beaming. Unlike laser beams, microwaves can penetrate clouds rainfall, making them prime candidate maximizing solar capacity.
Still,  engineering hurdles. Though Mr. Hajimiri' team Caltech proved wireless energy transfer microwaves space possible - even beamed detectable amount energy Earth -  did  beam enough power Earth convert  usable form.
"No  demonstrated power beaming  few kilometers," said Paul Jaffe, U.. Naval Research Laboratory engineer specializing power-beaming technology. Mr. Hajimiri thinks can done. Caltech engineer says working technologies would enable large array lightweight, sail-like spacecraft, using billions small transmitting antennas, create focused beam could travel thousands kilometers Earth carry megawatts worth energy.
scale space-based solar power structures  daunting. most prominent building space today International Space Station,  measures 357 feet end end. Space-based solar power systems would several thousand feet wide, army robots would needed autonomously assemble structures while orbit.
addition overcoming technical challenges, researchers must  ensure safety wirelessly beaming power Earth. Microwave laser beams pose known risk human health operated certain power densities. Researchers say power density space-based solar would designed operate within limits set international governing bodies. Still, no studies focused effect space-based beaming human health, environment atmosphere - critical step public acceptance technology.
Then, inevitably,   regulatory challenges. transmission radio waves orbit - including telecommunication, GPS weather satellites - requires licensing prevent interference different users. Solar-power satellites would likely need approval International Telecommunications Union, United Nations agency, protect license  operating frequencies.
complexity these challenges places expected arrival most space-based solar power projects 2030s 2040s, should  ever get point. That'  stopping researchers pressing forward dream harnessing uninterrupted, inexhaustible supply energy space.
